questions.question: Given the following cash flow stream: End of Year Annual Cash Flow $4,000 1 $2,000 2 3 -0- -$1,000 4 Using a 10% discount rate, the present value of this cash flow stream is:

How much should an investor have in a retirement account on his 65 birthday if he wishes to withdraw $40,000 on that birthday and each of the following 14 birthdays, assuming his retirement account is expected to earn 14.5%?
questions.explanation: This is an annuity due so set your calculator to the BGN mode. N = 15; I/Y = 14.5; PMT = - 40,000; FV = 0; CPT → PV = 274,422.50. Switch back to END mode.

questions.question: A local bank advertises that it will pay interest at the rate of 4.5%, compounded monthly, on regular savings accounts. What is the effective rate of interest that the bank is paying on these accounts?
A stated interest rate of 9% compounded quarterly results in an effective annual ratelosest to:
questions.explanation: Quarterly rate = 0.09 / 4 = 0.0225. Effective annual rate = (1 + 0.02255 - 1 = 0.09308, or 9.308%.

questions.question: It will cost $20,000 a year for four years when an 8-year old child is ready for college. How much should be invested today if the child will make the first of four annual withdrawals 10- years from today? The expected rate of return is 8%.
questions.explanation: First, find the present value of the college costs as of the end of year 9. (Remember that the PV of an ordinary annuity is as of time = 0. If the first payment is in year 10, then the present value of the annuity is indexed to the end of year 9). N = 4; I/Y = 8; PMT = 20,000; CPT → PV = $66,242.54. Second, find the present value of this single sum: N = 9; I/Y = 8; FV = 66,242.54; PMT = 0; CPT→ PV = 33,137.76.

questions.question: What is the effective annual rate if the stated rate is 12% compounded quarterly?
questions.explanation: If the stated rate is 12%, then the effective quarterly (period) rate is 12% / 4 = 3% The effective annual rate is, therefore, (1 + period rate periods in a year - 1 EAR = [1 + (0.12 | 4)F - 1 = 12.55%

questions.question: If $2,000 a year is invested at the end of each of the next 45 years in a retirement account yielding 8.5%, the amount the investor will have after 45 years islosestto:

Wortel Industries has preferred stock outstanding that paying an annual dividend of $3.75 per share. If an investor wants to earn a rate of return of 8.5%, how much should he be willing to pay for a share of Wortel preferred stock?
questions.explanation: To calculate the price, we need to discount the future dividend stream at the investor's required return. The stream of dividends is a perpetuity (a fixed dividend each year forever). Given the PV of a perpetuity = cash flow / discount rate Then price = $3.75 / 0.085 = $44.12

questions.question: Selmer Jones has just inherited some money and wants to set some of it aside for a vacation in Hawaii one year from today. His bank will pay him 5% interest on any funds he deposits. In order to determine how much of the money must be set aside and held for the trip, he should use the 5% as a:
questions.explanation: He needs to figure out how much the trip will cost in one year, and use the 5% as a discount rate to convert the future cost to a present value. Thus, in this context the rate is best viewed as a discount rate.

questions.question: The future value a 10-year annuity paying an annual sum of $10,000 at the end of each year given a discount rate of 10% would be:
An investor makes 48 monthly payments of $500 each beginning today into an account that will have a value of $29,000 at the end of four years. The stated annual interest rate is closest to.
questions.explanation: Because this is an annuity due (payments at the start of each period) the calculator must first be set to BGN mode. N = 48; PMT = 500; FV = -29,000; PV = 0; CPT I/Y = 0.7532 This percentage is a monthly rate because the time periods were entered as 48 months. It must be converted to a stated annual percentage rate (APR) by multiplying by the number of compounding periods per year: 0.7532 × 12 = 9.04%.

questions.question: Given investors require an annual return of 12.5%, a perpetual bond (i.e., a bond with no maturity/due date) that pays $87.50 a year in interest should be valued at:

Five years ago, an investor borrowed $5,000 from a financial institution that charged a 6% annual interest rate, and he immediately took his family to live in Nepal. He made no payments during the time he was away. When he returned, he agreed to repay the original loan plus the accrued interest by making five end-of-year payments starting one year after he returned. If the interest rate on the loan is held constant at 6% per year, what annual payment must the invstor make in order to retire the loan?
questions.explanation: With no interest paid on the original $5,000 loan, at 6% in five years the loan balance will be: New loan balance = $5,000(1.065 = $6,691.13 or PV = 5,000; |/Y = 6; N = 5; PMT = O; CPT → FV = -$6,691.13. $6,691.13 is the loan that has to be retired over the next five years. The financial calculator solution is: PV = 6,691.13; |/Y = 6; N = 5; FV = 0; CPT→ PMT. You obtain PMT = -1,588.45.

questions.question: A firm is evaluating an investment that promises to generate the following annual cash flows: End of Year Cash Flows 1 $5,000 $5,000 2 $5,000 3 $5,000 4 $5,000 5 6 -0- 7 -0- $2,000 8 $2,000 9 Given BBC uses an 8% discount rate, this investment should be valued at:
If 10 equal annual deposits of $1,000 are made into an investment account earning 9% starting today, how much will you have in 20 years?
questions.explanation: Switch to BGN mode. PMT = -1,000; N = 10, I/Y = 9, PV = 0; CPT FV = 16,560.29. Remember the answer will be one year after the last payment in annuity due FV problems. Now PV10 = 16,560.29; N = 10; I/Y = 9; PMT = 0; CPT→ FV = 39,204.23. Switch back to END mode.

questions.question: An investor wants to receive $1,000 at the beginning of each of the next ten years with the first payment starting today. If the investor can earn 10 percent interest, what must the investor put into the account today in order to receive this $1,000 cash flow stream?
questions.explanation: This is an annuity due problem. There are several ways to solve this problem. Method 1: PV of first $1,000 = $1,000 PV of next 9 payments at 10% = 5,759.02 Sum of payments = $6,759.02 Method 2: Put calculator in BGN mode. N = 10; I = 10; PMT = -1,000; CPT→ PV = 6,759.02 Note: make PMT negative to get a positive PV. Don't forget to take your calculator out of BGN mode. Method 3: You can also find the present value of the ordinary annuity $6,144.57 and multiply by 1 + k to add one year of interest to each cash flow. $6,144.57 × 1.1 = $6,759.02.

questions.question: Concerning an ordinary annuity and an annuity due with the same payments and positive interest rate, which of the following statements ismost accurate?

options.A: The present value of the ordinary annuity is greater than an annuity due.
options.B: The present value of the ordinary annuity is less than an annuity due.

questions.explanation: With a positive interest rate, the present value of an ordinary annuity is less than the present value of an annuity due. The first cash flow in an annuity due is at the beginning of the period, while in an ordinary annuity, the first cash flow occurs at the end of the period. Therefore, each cash flow of the ordinary annuity is discounted one period more.

questions.question: As the number of compounding periods increases, what is the effect on the EAR? EAR:
questions.explanation: There is an upper limit to the EAR as the frequency of compounding increases. In the limit, with continuous compounding the EAR = APR -1. Hence, the EAR increases at a decreasing rate.

questions.question: A $500 investment offers a 7.5% annual rate of return. How much will it be worth in four years?
What is the present value of a 10-year, $100 annual annuity due if interest rates are 0%?
questions.explanation: When I/Y = 0 you just sum up the numbers since there is no interest earned.

questions.question: Wei Zhang has funds on deposit with Iron Range bank. The funds are currently earning 6% interest. If he withdraws $15,000 to purchase an automobile, the 6% interest rate can be best thought of as a(n):
questions.explanation: Since Wei will be foregoing interest on the withdrawn funds, the 6% interest can be best characterized as an opportunity cost — the return he could earn by postponing his auto purchase until the future.

questions.question: Compute the present value of a perpetuity with $100 payments beginning four years from now. Assume the appropriate annual interest rate is 10%.
questions.explanation: Compute the present value of the perpetuity at (t = 3). Recall, the present value of a perpetuity or annuity is valued one period before the first payment. So, the present value at t = 3 is 100 / 0.10 = 1,000. Now it is necessary to discount this lump sum to t = 0. Therefore, present value at t = 0 is 1,000 / (1.10) = 751.

questions.question: What is the maximum an investor should be willing to pay for an annuity that will pay out $10,000 at the beginning of each of the next 10 years, given the investor wants to earn 12.5%, compounded annually?
questions.explanation: Using END mode, the PV of this annuity due is $10,000 plus the present value of a 9-year ordinary annuity: N=9; I/Y=12.5; PMT=-10,000; FV=0; CPT PV=$52,285; $52,285 + $10,000 = $62,285. Or set your calculator to BGN mode then N=10; I/Y=12.5; PMT=-10,000; FV=0; CPT PV= $62,285.

questions.question: A local bank offers an account that pays 8%, compounded quarterly, for any deposits of $10,000 or more that are left in the account for a period of 5 years. The effective annual rate of interest on this account is:
In 10 years, what is the value of $100 invested today at an interest rate of 8% per year, compounded monthly?

Three years from now, an investor will deposit the first of eight $1,000 payments into a special fund. The fund will earn interest at the rate of 5% per year until the third deposit is made. Thereafter, the fund will return a reduced interest rate of 4% compounded annually until the final deposit is made. How much money will the investor have in the fund at the end of ten years assuming no withdrawals are made?
questions.explanation: It's best to break this problem into parts to accommodate the change in the interest rate. Money in the fund at the end of ten years based on deposits made with initial interest of 5%: (1) The total value in the fund at the end of the fifth year is $3,152.50: PMT = - 1,000; N = 3; I/Y =5; CPT→ FV = $3,152.50. (calculator in END mode) (2) The $3,152.50 is now the present value and will then grow at 4% until the end of the tenth year. We get: PV = -3,152.50; N = 5; I/Y = 4; PMT = - 1,000; CPT→ FV = $9,251.82

questions.question: If an investment has an APR of 18% and is compounded quarterly, its effective annual rate (EAR) is closest to.
questions.explanation: Because this investment is compounded quarterly, we need to divide the APR by four compounding periods: 18 / 4 = 4.5%. EAR = (1.045) - 1 = 0.1925, or 19.25%.

questions.question: The First State Bank is willing to lend $100,000 for 4 years at 12%. Assuming the loan is fully amortizing repayable in semiannual installments, the first payment islosestto:
questions.explanation: The loan is repayable over 8 six-month periods. The rate of interest over six months is 12% / 2 = 6%. Given the present value of the loan is $100,000, we need to calculate the six monthly annuity that arises. Using the calculator: N = 8; |/Y = 6; PV = -100,000; CPT→ PMT = 16,103.59.

questions.question: If a $45,000 car loan is financed at 12% over 4 years, what is the monthly car payment?

A local bank offers a certificate of deposit (CD) that earns 5.0% compounded quarterly for three and one half years. If a depositor places $5,000 on deposit, what will be the value of the account at maturity?
questions.explanation: The value of the account at maturity will be: $5,000 x (1 + 0.05 / 43.5 x 4) = $5.949.77; or with a financial calculator: N = 3 years × 4 quarters/year + 2 = 14 periods; | = 5% / 4 quarters/year = 1.25; PV = $5,000; PMT = 0; CPT→ FV = $5,949.77.

questions.question: Paul Kohler inherits $50,000 and deposits it immediately in a bank account that pays 6% interest. No other deposits or withdrawals are made. In two years, what will be the account balance assuming monthly compounding?
questions.explanation: To compound monthly, remember to divide the interest rate by 12 (6%/12 = 0.50%) and the number of periods will be 2 years times 12 months (2 × 12 = 24 periods). The value after 24 periods is $50,000 × 1.0054 = $56,357.99. The problem can also be solved using the time value of money functions: N = 24; |/Y = 0.5; PMT = 0; PV = 50,000; CPT FV = $56,357.99.

questions.question: If $2,500 is invested at the end of each year for the next 10 years, earning 15% interest compounded annually, the value at the beginning of the eleventh year is belosestto:
questions.explanation: The beginning of the eleventh year is the same point in time as the end of the tenth year. So we can perform the calculation as if the question were asking for a value at the end of the tenth year. N = 10; | = 15; PMT = 2,500; CPT→ FV = $50,759.

questions.question: Suppose you are going to deposit $1,000 at the start of this year, $1,500 at the start of next year, and $2,000 at the start of the following year in a savings account. How much money will you have at the end of three years if the rate of interest is 10% each year?
questions.explanation: Future value of $1,000 for 3 periods at 10% = 1,331 Future value of $1,500 for 2 periods at 10% = 1,815 Future value of $2,000 for 1 period at 10% = 2,200 Total = $5,346 N = 3; PV = -$1,000; |/Y = 10%; CPT→ FV = $1,331 N = 2; PV = -$1,500; I/Y = 10%; CPT→ FV = $1,815 N = 1; PV = -$2,000; |/Y = 10%; CPT→ FV = $2,200

questions.question: An investor has the choice of two investments. Investment A offers interest at 7.25% compounded quarterly. Investment B offers interest at the annual rate of 7.40%. Which investment offers the higher dollar return on an investment of $50,000 for two years, and by how much?

options.A: Investment A offers a $122.18 greater return.
options.B: Investment B offers a $36.92 greater return.
options.C: Investment A offers a $53.18 greater return.

questions.explanation: Investment A: | = 7.25 / 4; N = 2 × 4 = 8; PV = $50,000; PMT = 0; CPT> FV = $57,726.98 Investment B: 1 = 7.40; N = 2; PV = $50,000; PMT = 0; CPT > FV = $57,673.80 Difference = investment A offers a $53.18 greater dollar return.

questions.question: Justin Banks just won the lottery and is trying to decide between the annual cash flow payment option or the lump sum option. He can earn 8% at the bank and the annual cash flow option is $100,000/year, beginning today for 15 years. What is the annual cash flow option worth to Banks today?
questions.explanation: First put your calculator in the BGN. N = 15; I/Y = 8; PMT = 100,000; CPT→ PV = 924,423.70. Alternatively, do not set your calculator to BGN, simply multiply the ordinary annuity (end of the period payments) answer by 1 + I/Y. You get the annuity due answer and you don't run the risk of forgetting to reset your calculator back to the end of the period setting. OR N = 14; |/Y = 8; PMT = 100,000; CPT→ PV = 824,423.70 + 100,000 = 924,423.70.

questions.question: A successful investor has decided to set up a scholarship fund for deserving students at her alma mater. Her plan is for the fund to be capable of awarding $25,000 annually in perpetuity. The first scholarship is to be awarded and paid out exactly four years from today. The funds will be deposited into an account immediately and will grow at a rate of 4%, compounded semiannually, for the foreseeable future. How much money must the investor donate today to fund the scholarship?
questions.explanation: The investor has to ensure that the amount deposited now will grow into the amount needed to fund the perpetuity. With semiannual compounding, the effective annual rate (EAR) earned on funds in the account is: 0.01)? annual rate ) - 1- (1+= EAR = (1+ = - 1 = 0.0404 = 4.04% The present value of the perpetuity = $25,000/0.0404 = $618,811.88. Note that since the first scholarship award is paid out in four years, the present value of the perpetuity represents the amount that must be in the account at time t = 3. We can find the required deposit from: 618,811.88 FV = -618,811.88; N = 3; I = 4.04; CPT→ PV = $549,487.24 or - = $549,487.24 1.04043

questions.question: An investor purchases a 10-year, $1,000 par value bond that pays annual coupons of $100. If the market rate of interest is 12%, what is the current market value of the bond?
questions.explanation: Note that bond problems are just mixed annuity problems. You can solve bond problems directly with your financial calculator using all five of the main TVM keys at once. For bond- types of problems the bond's price (PV) will be negative, while the coupon payment (PMT) and par value (FV) will be positive. N= 10; I/Y = 12; FV = 1,000; PMT = 100; CP7› PV = -

questions.question: Renee Fisher invests $2,000 each year, starting one year from now, in a retirement account. If the investments earn 8% or 10% annually over 30 years, the amount Fisher will accumulate is closestto: 10% 8%
Given an 8.5% discount rate, an asset that generates cash flows of $10 in Year 1, -$20 in Year 2, $10 in Year 3, and is then sold for $150 at the end of Year 4, has a present value of:
questions.explanation: Using your cash flow keys, C5 = 0; CF1 = 10; CF2 = -20; CF3 = 10; CF4 = 150; |/Y = 8.5; NPV = $108.29.

questions.question: An investor will receive an annuity of $5,000 a year for seven years. The first payment is to be received 5 years from today. If the annual interest rate is 11.5%, what is the present value of the annuity?

Sarah Parker is buying a new $25,000 car. Her trade-in is worth $5,000 so she needs to borrow $20,000. The loan will be paid in 48 monthly installments and the annual interest rate on the loan is 7.5%. If the first payment is due at the end of the first month, what is Sarah's monthly car payment?

Optimal Insurance is offering a deferred annuity that promises to pay 10% per annum with equal annual payments beginning at the end of 10 years and continuing for a total of 10 annual payments. For an initial investment of $100,000, the amount of the annual payments will be closestto:
questions.explanation: The time line for the cash flows is as follows: 1 2 45 6 7 12 13 15 16 17 18 19 ? $100,000 One period before the deferred annuity begins (i.e., at t = 9), the value will be: $100,000 x (1 + 0.10)9 = $235,794.77. Using a financial calculator and solving for a 10-year annuity: N = 10; I/Y = 10; PV = -235,794.77; FV = 0; CPT PMT = 38,374.51

questions.question: An annuity will pay eight annual payments of $100, with the first payment to be received one year from now. If the interest rate is 12% per year, what is the present value of this annuity?
What is the present value of $200 to be received one year from now, $300 to be received 3 years from now, and $600 to be received 5 years from now assuming an interest rate of 5%?
questions.explanation: 200 / (1.05) + 300/ (1.05} + 600/ (1.05F = 919.74. This can also be solved using the net present value function: CF = 0; C01 = 200; C02 = 0; C03 = 300; C04 = 0; C05 = 600; | = 5; CPT NPV = 919.74

questions.question: A local loan shark offers 4 for 5 on payday. What it involves is that you borrow $4 from him and repay $5 on the next payday (one week later). What would the stated annual interest rate be on this loan, with weekly compounding? Assuming 52 weeks in one year, what is the effective annual interest rate on this loan? Select the respective answer choices closest to your numbers.
questions.explanation: Stated Weekly Rate = 5/4 - 1 = 25% Stated Annual Rate = 1,300% Annual Effective Interest Rate = (1 + 0.2552 - 1 = 109,476.44 - 1 = 10,947,544%

questions.question: T-bill yields can be thought of as:

options.A: nominal risk-free rates because they contain an inflation premium.
options.B: nominal risk-free rates because they do not contain an inflation premium.
options.C: real risk-free rates because they contain an inflation premium.

questions.explanation: T-bills are government issued securities and are therefore considered to be default risk free. More precisely, they are nominal risk-free rates rather than real risk-free rates since they contain a premium for expected inflation.

questions.question: Jamie Morgan needs to accumulate $2,000 in 18 months. If she can earn 6% at the bank, compounded quarterly, how much must she deposit today?
questions.explanation: Each quarter of a year is comprised of 3 months thus N = 18 / 3 = 6; |/Y = 6 / 4 = 1.5; PMT = 0; FV = 2,000; CPT → PV = $1,829.08.

questions.question: A major brokerage house is currently selling an investment product that offers an 8% rate of return, compounded monthly. Based on this information, it follows that this investment has:
questions.explanation: Periodic rate = 8.0 / 12 = 0.667. Stated rate is 8.0% and effective rate is 8.30%.

questions.question: Lois Weaver wants to accumulate $1.5 million in a retirement fund when she retires in 30 years. If Weaver can earn a 9% rate of return on her investments, the amount she must invest at the end of each year for 30 years to reach her goal islosestto:
questions.explanation: Using a financial calculator: N = 30; I/Y = 9; FV = -1,500,000; PV = 0; CPT› PMT = 11,004.52.

questions.question: Given a 5% discount rate, the present value of $500 to be received three years from today is:

Steve Hall wants to give his son a new car for his graduation. If the cost of the car is $15,000 and Hall finances 80% of the value of the car for 36 months at 8% annual interest, his monthly payments will be:
The future value of $1,000 invested for one year at a rate of interest of 12% compounded monthly is closestto:
questions.explanation: The monthly interest rate is 12% / 12 = 1%. The future value after 12 months will be $1,000 × (1.01)12 = $1,126.83.

questions.question: The value in 7 years of $500 invested today at an interest rate of 6% compounded monthly is closest to:
A share of George Co. preferred stock is selling for $65. It pays a dividend of $4.50 per year and has a perpetual life. The rate of return it is offering its investors islosestto:

An investor who requires an annual return of 12% has the choice of receiving one of the following: Option A: 10 annual payments of $1,225.00 to begin at the end of one year. Option B: 10 annual payments of $1,097.96 beginning immediately. Which option has the highest present value (PV) and approximately how much greater is it than the other option?

options.A: Option A's PV is $42 greater than option B's.
options.B: Option B's PV is $114 greater than option A's.
options.C: Option B's PV is $27 greater than option A's.

questions.explanation: Option A: N = 10, PMT = -$1,225, I = 12%, FV = 0, Compute PV = $6,921.52. Option B: N = 9, PMT = -$1,097.96, I = 12%, FV = 0, Compute PV > $5,850.51 + 1,097.96 = 6,948.17 or put calculator in Begin mode N = 10, PMT = $1,097.96, I = 12%, FV = 0, Compute PV → $6,948.17. Difference between the 2 options = $6,921.52 - $6,948.17 = -$26.65. Option B's PV is approximately $27 higher than option A's PV.

questions.question: What is the compound annual growth rate for stock A which has annual returns of 5.60%, 22.67%, and -5.23%?
questions.explanation: Compound annual growth rate is the geometric mean. (1.056 × 1.2267 × 0.9477/3 - 1 = 7.08%

questions.question: A linear or nonlinear relationship between two variables is best visualized using a:
questions.explanation: A scatter plot is useful for visualizing the relationship between two variables. An advantage of scatter plots is that they can reveal nonlinear relationships that measures of linear relationship such as correlation might not show.

questions.question: An investment experienced the following returns over the last 10 years: Year Return 2% 2 9% 3 8% 4 -5% 5 6% 6 8% 9% 7 8 -3% 9 10% 10 3% Using a target return of 4%, the target semideviation of returns over the period islosestto:
If Stock X's expected return is 30% and its expected standard deviation is 5%, Stock x's expected coefficient of variation is:
questions.explanation: The coefficient of variation is the standard deviation divided by the mean: 5 / 30 = 0.167.

questions.question: An investor has a portfolio with 10% cash, 30% bonds, and 60% stock. If last year's return on cash was 2.0%, the return on bonds was 9.5%, and the return on stock was 25%, what was the return on the investor's portfolio?
questions.explanation: Find the weighted mean of the returns. (0.10 x 0.02) + (0.30 x 0.095) + (0,60 × 0.25) = 18.05% Asset Weight Return Weight × Return Cash 10% 2% 10% × 2% = 0.2% 9.5% Bonds 30% 30% × 9.5% = 2.85% Stock 60% 25% 60% × 25% = 15% Weighted Average Return 18.05% [Weight × Probability

questions.question: Which of the following is most likely an example of structured data?

options.B: Management's discussion and analysis of a company's financial condition.
options.C: Daily closing prices for a stock over the past month.

questions.explanation: Daily closing prices for a stock are an example of structured data. Social media posts and the management discussion and analysis are examples of unstructured data, in that they consist largely of written text.

questions.question: The sample of per square foot sales for 100 U.S. retailers in December 2004 is an example of:
questions.explanation: Cross-sectional data are a sample of observations taken at a single point in time. A time- series is a sample of observations taken at specific and equally spaced points in time. Panel data consist of a cross-section of time series data.

questions.question: Use the results from the following survey of 500 firms to answer the question. Number of Employees Frequency 300 up to 400 40 400 up to 500 62 78 500 up to 600 101 600 up to 700 700 up to 800 131 800 up to 900 88 The cumulative relative frequency of the second interval (400 to 500) is:
If a distribution is positively skewed, then generally:
questions.explanation: When a distribution is positively skewed the right side tail is longer than normal due to outliers. The mean will exceed the median, and the median will generally exceed the mode because large outliers falling to the far right side of the distribution can dramatically influence the mean.

questions.question: A scatter plot matrix is best suited to visualize:

options.A: trends in more than one variable over time.
options.C: the joint variability between two variables.

questions.explanation: A scatter plot matrix is a set of scatter plots that is useful for visualizing correlations among multiple pairs of variables.

questions.question: Which of the following tools is best suited for visualizing the relative changes over time in the daily closing prices for two stocks?
questions.explanation: A line chart is a graph used to visualize ordered observations such as data series over time. Two or more lines can appear on the same chart to show the relative changes in variables.

questions.question: Twenty students take an exam. The percentages of questions they answer correctly are ranked from lowest to highest as follows: 57 58 61 32 49 62 64 66 67 67 68 69 71 72 72 74 76 80 82 83 In a frequency distribution from 30% to 90% that is divided into six equal-sized intervals, the absolute frequency of the sixth interval is:
questions.explanation: The intervals are 30%≤ x < 40%, 40% ≤ x < 50%, 50% ≤ x < 60%, 60% ≤ x < 70%, 70% ≤ x < 80%, and 80% ≤ x ≤ 90%. There are 3 scores in the range 80%≤ x ≤ 90%.

questions.question: An analyst observes the following four annual returns: R = +10%, Rz = -15%, R3 = 0%, and R4 = +5%. The average compound annual rate over the four years islosestto:
questions.explanation: G = [(1.10)(0.85)(1.00)(1.05)p.25 - 1 G = (0.981750.25 - 1 = 0.9954 - 1 = -0.00459= -0.5% Note: Taking a number to the 0.25 power is the same as taking the fourth root of the

questions.question: Michael Philizaire decides to calculate the geometric average of the appreciation/deprecation of his home over the last five years. Using comparable sales and market data he obtains from a local real estate appraiser, Phillizaire calculates the year-to- year percentage change in the value of his home as follows: 20, 15, 0, -5, -5. The geometric return is closestto:
A sample of returns for four randomly selected assets in a portfolio is shown below: Asset Return (%) 1.3 A 1.4 B 2.2 3.4 D What is the sample standard deviation of asset returns?
questions.explanation: The sample standard deviation equals the square root of the sum of the squares of the position returns less the mean return, divided by the number of observations in the sample minus one. Position Return (%) (Return - Mean} 1.3 0.60 1.4 0.46 2.2 0.02 C 3.4 1.76 D 8.3/4 = 2.075 Sum = 2.83 Mean Std. Dev. = [2.83 / (4 - 1)P.5 = 0.97

questions.question: Twenty Level | CFA candidates in a study group took a practice exam and want to determine the distribution of their scores. When they grade their exams they discover that one of them skipped an ethics question and subsequently filled in the rest of his answers in the wrong places, leaving him with a much lower score than the rest of the group. If they include this candidate's score, their distribution willmost likely.

options.A: have a mean that is less than its median.
options.B: have a mode that is less than its median.

questions.explanation: With the low outlier included, the distribution will be negatively skewed. For a negatively skewed distribution, the mean is less than the median, which is less than the mode.

questions.question: Trina Romel, mutual fund manager, is taking over a poor-performing fund from a colleague. Romel wants to calculate the return on the portfolio. Over the last five years, the fund's annual percentage returns were: 25, 15, 12, -8, and -14 Determine if the geometric return of the fund will be less than or greater than the arithmetic return and calculate the fund's geometric return: Geometric compared to Geometric Return Arithmetic greater than
questions.explanation: The geometric return is calculated as follows: [(1 + 0.25)(1 + 0.15)(1 + 0.12)(1 - 0.08)(1-0.14)915 - 1, or [1.25 × 1.15 × 1.12 × 0.92 × 0.869.2 - 1 = 0.4960, or 4.96%. The geometric return will always be less than or equal to the arithmetic return. In this case the arithmetic return was 6%.

questions.question: An analyst takes a sample of yearly returns of aggressive growth funds resulting in the following data set: 25, 15, 35, 45, and 55. The mean absolute deviation (MAD) of the data set is closest to:
questions.explanation: Calculate the mean: 25+15+35+45+55 = 35 5 To get the mean absolute deviation, sum the deviations around the mean (ignoring the sign), and divide by the number of observations: 10+20+0+10+20 = 12 5

questions.question: Which of the following statements concerning a distribution with positive skewness and positive excess kurtosis is/east accurate?

options.A: The mean will be greater than the mode. It has a lower percentage of small deviations from the mean than a normal distribution.
options.C: It has fatter tails than a normal distribution.

questions.explanation: A distribution with positive excess kurtosis has a higher percentage of small deviations from the mean than normal. So it is more "peaked" than a normal distribution. A distribution with positive skew has a mean > mode.

questions.question: What does it mean to say that an observation is at the sixty-fifth percentile?

options.A: The observation falls within the 65th of 100 intervals.
options.B: 65% of all the observations are above that observation.
options.C: 65% of all the observations are below that observation.

questions.explanation: If the observation falls at the sixty-fifth percentile, 65% of all the observations fall below that observation.

questions.question: The correlation between two variables is -0.74. Themost appropriateway to interpret this correlation is that:

options.A: there is unlikely to be a strong linear relationship between the two variables. x if one of the variables increases, there is a 74% probability that the other
options.C: the two variables have a negative linear association.

questions.explanation: A correlation coefficient of -0.74 suggests a relatively strong negative linear association between the two variables. We cannot interpret the correlation coefficient directly as a measure of the probability that the two variables will change in opposite directions.

questions.question: For the last four years, the returns for XYZ Corporation's stock have been 10.4%, 8.1%, 3.2%, and 15.0%. The equivalent compound annual rate is:
A company reports its past six years' earnings growth at 10%, 14%, 12%, 10%, -10%, and 12%. The company's average compound annual growth rate of earnings islosestto:
Which of the following tools best captures the distribution of returns for a particular stock?
questions.explanation: A histogram depicts the shape and range of a distribution of numerical data.

questions.question: The owner of a company has recently decided to raise the salary of one employee, who was already making the highest salary in the company, by 40%. Which of the following value(s) is (are) expected to be affected by this raise?
questions.explanation: Mean is affected because it is the sum of all values / number of observations. Median is not affected as it the midpoint between the top half of values and the bottom half of values.

questions.question: Given the following annual returns, what is the mean absolute deviation? 2000 2001 2002 2003 2004 15% 2% 5% -7% 0%
questions.explanation: The mean absolute deviation is found by taking the mean of the absolute values of deviation from the mean. ( | 15 - 3| + 12 - 31 + 15- 31 + 1-7-31+10-31/5 = 5.60%

questions.question: An analyst compiles the returns on Fund Q over the last four years: Year Return 1 4% 2 3% 3 2% 4 30% Which of the following will result in thelowest measure of the mean return?

The following annualized monthly return measures have been calculated for an investment based on its performance over the last 72 months. Arithmetic mean 6.8% Geometric mean 6.0% 90% Winsorized mean 5.5% If for one month in the period the return was extremely high, which measurbest reflects the central tendency of the investment's returns?
questions.explanation: A winsorized mean is a technique for removing the distorting effects of outliers by replacing them with less extreme values. The arithmetic and geometric means are based on all observations and therefore include the impact of outliers.

questions.question: In a frequency distribution histogram, the frequency of an interval is given by the:

options.A: height multiplied by the width of the corresponding bar.

questions.explanation: In a histogram, intervals are placed on the horizontal axis, and frequencies are placed on the vertical axis. The frequency of a particular interval is given by the value on the vertical axis, or the height of the corresponding bar.

questions.question: An investor has the following assets: • $5,000 in bonds with an expected return of 8%. • $10,000 in equities with an expected return of 12%. • $5,000 in real estate with an expected return of 10%. What is the portfolio's expected return?
questions.explanation: Expected return is the weighted average of the individual expected values. The expected return is: [5,000)x (10.00) + (5,000) x (8.00) + (10,000) × (12.00)] / 20,000 = 10.50%.

questions.question: The respective arithmetic mean and geometric mean returns of the following series of stock market returns are: Year 1 | 14% Year 2 6% Year 3-5% Year 4 20%
Which of the following statements concerning skewness iseast accurate? A distribution with:

options.A: skew equal to 1 is not symmetrical.
options.B: negative skewness has a large number of outliers on its left side.
options.C: positive skewness has a long left tail.

questions.explanation: A distribution with positive skewness has longright tails.

questions.question: A distribution of returns that has a greater percentage of small deviations from the mean and a greater percentage of large deviations from the mean compared to a normal distribution:
questions.explanation: A distribution that has a greater percentage of small deviations from the mean and a greater percentage of large deviations from the mean will be leptokurtic and will exhibit positive excess kurtosis. The distribution will be taller (more peaked) with fatter tails than a normal distribution.

questions.question: If the historical mean return on an investment is 2.0%, the standard deviation is 8.8%, and the risk free rate is 0.5%, what is the coefficient of variation (CV)?
questions.explanation: The CV = the standard deviation of returns / mean return = 8.8% / 2.0% = 4.4. The CV is a measure of risk per unit of mean return. When ranking portfolios based on the CV, a lower value is preferred to higher.

questions.question: What are the median and the third quintile of the following data points, respectively? 9.2%, 10.1%, 11.5%, 11.9%, 12.2%, 12.8%, 13.1%, 13.6%, 13.9%, 14.2%, 14.8%, 14.9%, 15.4%
questions.explanation: The median is the midpoint of the data points. In this case there are 13 data points and the midpoint is the th term. The formula for determining quantiles is: 4 = (n + 1)(y) / (100). Here, we are looking for the third quintile (60% of the observations lie below) and the formula is: (14)(60) / (100) = 8.4. The third quintile falls between 13.6% and 13.9%, the 8' and 9th numbers from the left. Since L is not a whole number, we interpolate as: 0.136 + (0.40)(0.139 - 0.136) = 0.1372, or 13.7%.

questions.question: For the investments shown in the table below: Investment Return (%) 12 A 14 9 13 E 7 F 8 12 G Which of the following statements ismost accurate

options.A: The mean is equal to the median.
options.B: The mean is equal to the mode.
options.C: The median is equal to the mode.

questions.explanation: The median is the mid-point or central number of returns arranged from highest to lowest or lowest to highest. In this case: 7, 8, 9,12, 12, 13, 14. The median return is 12%. The mode is the return that occurs most frequently. In this case, 12% is also the mode. The mean is 75 / 7 = 10.71%.

questions.question: A scatter plot is best interpreted as displaying the: 8

options.A: relationship of a dependent variable with an independent variable.
options.C: behavior of a variable over time.

questions.explanation: Scatter plots display paired observations of two variables. They do not require the two variables to have a dependent relationship, but they can be a tool for analyzing whether one of the variables is dependent on the other.

questions.question: Which of the following statements about kurtosis iseast accurate? Kurtosis: measures the peakedness of a distribution reflecting a greater or lesser

options.A: concentration of returns around the mean.
options.B: describes the degree to which a distribution is not symmetric about its mean.
options.C: is used to reflect the probability of extreme outcomes for a return distribution.

questions.explanation: The degree to which a distribution is not symmetric about its mean is measured by skewness. Excess kurtosis which is measured relative to a normal distribution, indicates the peakedness of a distribution, and also reflects the probability of extreme outcomes.

questions.question: Market Capitalization Style Small-cap stocks Large-cap stocks 20 Value stocks 5 50 Growth stocks 25 In the contingency table above, the values shown are:
questions.explanation: The values shown in the table are joint frequencies. For example, 20 is the joint frequency of large-cap stocks and value stocks. Marginal frequencies and relative frequencies can be calculated from this table. For example, the marginal frequency of growth stocks is 25 + 50 = 75, and the relative frequency of value stocks among small-cap stocks is 5 / (5 + 25) = 16.7%.

questions.question: Over the last five years, an investment fund's monthly returns were relatively stable apart from last year, where two extremely high returns were recorded. If the arithmetic mean for the fund's monthly returns over the period is 6.7%, a trimmed or winsorized mean return is most likelyto be:
questions.explanation: A trimmed mean discards a percentage of the highest and lowest observations, while a winsorized mean replaces a percentage of the highest and lowest observations with less extreme values. In this case the arithmetic mean would be influenced by the two highly positive returns, while a trimmed or winsorized mean would adjust for them and would likely be lower than the arithmetic mean.

questions.question: The following data points are observed returns. 4.2%, 6.8%, 7.0%, 10.9%, 11.6%, 14.4%, 17.0%, 19.0%, 22.5% What return lies at the 70th percentile (70% of returns lie below this return)?
questions.explanation: With 9 observations, the location of the 70th percentile is (9 + 1)(70 / 100) = 7. The seventh observation in ascending order is 17.0%.

questions.question: A two-dimensional array is most appropriate for organizing data on annual earnings per share for:

options.A: 100 companies in the most recent period.
options.B: ABC Ltd over the last five years.
options.C: banking sector companies over the last three years.

questions.explanation: "Banking sector companies" and "the last three years" represent two dimensions. One company's earnings per share over the last five years is a time series, and earnings per share for 100 companies in the most recent period are a cross section, either of which require only a one-dimensional array.

questions.question: In a positively skewed distribution, what is the order (from lowest value to highest) for the distribution's mode, mean, and median values?
questions.explanation: In a positively skewed distribution, the mode is less than the median, which is less than the mean.

questions.question: Consider the following set of stock returns: 12%, 23%, 27%, 10%, 7%, 20%, 15%. The third quartile is:
questions.explanation: The third quartile is calculated as: y = (7 + 1) (75/100) = 6. When we order the observations in ascending order: 7%, 10%, 12%, 15%, 20%, 23%, 27%, "23%" is the sixth observation from the left.

questions.question: Cameron Ryan wants to make an offer on the condominium he is renting. He takes a sample of prices of condominiums in his development that closed in the last five months. Sample prices are as follows (amounts are in thousands of dollars): $125, $175, $150, $155 and $135. The sample standard deviation isclosestto:
For a unimodal distribution with negative skewness:

options.A: the mode is less than the median.
options.B: the median is greater than the mean.
options.C: the mean is greater than the mode.

questions.explanation: For a distribution with negative skewness, mean < median < mode.

questions.question: The dividend yields for several stocks over each of the last five years are best characterized as:
questions.explanation: Panel data are a combination of time series and cross-sectional data. Dividend yields for several stocks are cross-sectional data, and data for each stock over the last five years are time series.

questions.question: Returns for a portfolio over the last four years are shown below. Treating these returns as a sample, what is their coefficient of variation (CV)? Year Return 17.0% 1 2 12.2% 3 3.9% -8.4% 4
questions.explanation: The coefficient of variation is equal to the standard deviation of returns divided by the mean return. questions.question: For a positively skewed distribution, the median is greater than:

options.A: the mean, but less than the mode.
options.B: the mode, but less than the mean.
options.C: both the mode and the mean.

questions.explanation: For a positively skewed distribution, the mean is greater than the median, and the median is greater than the mode. Their order reverses for a negatively skewed distribution.

questions.question: What is the seventh decile of the following data points? 84 81 91 97 102 108 110 112 115 |121 128 135 138 141 142 147 153 155 • 159| 162
questions.explanation: The formula for determining quantiles is: y = (n + 1)(y) / (100). Here, we are looking for the seventh decile (70% of the observations lie below) and the formula is: (21)(70) / (100) = 14.7. The seventh decile falls between 141.0 and 142.0, the fourteenth and fifteenth numbers from the left. Since L is not a whole number, we interpolate as: 141.0 + (0.70) (142.0 - 141.0) = 141.7.

questions.question: Given the following frequency distribution: Frequency Interval 10 up to 30 5 10 30 up to 50 15 50 up to 70 70 up to 90 5 Which of the following statements isleast accurate?

options.A: The number of observations is greater than 30.
options.B: The absolute frequency of the third interval is 15.
options.C: The relative frequency of the second interval is less than 15%.

questions.explanation: The relative interval frequency is (interval frequency) / (total number) = 28.57%. The number of observations is 5 + 10 + 15 + 5 = 35.

questions.question: An analyst is asked to calculate standard deviation using monthly returns over the last five years. These data are best described as:
questions.explanation: Time series data are taken at equally spaced intervals, such as monthly, quarterly, or annual. Cross sectional data are taken at a single point in time. An example of cross- sectional data is dividend yields on 500 stocks as of the end of a year.

questions.question: Which of the following statements concerning kurtosis ismost accurate?

options.A: A distribution with kurtosis of +2 has fatter tails than a normal distribution.
options.B: A leptokurtic distribution has excess kurtosis less than zero.
options.C: A leptokurtic distribution has fatter tails than a normal distribution.

questions.explanation: A leptokurtic distribution is more peaked than normal and has fatter tails. However, the excess kurtosis is greater than zero.

questions.question: Categorical or qualitative data types include:
questions.explanation: Ordinal data types are categorical (qualitative) data that can be ranked in logical order but cannot be subject to arithmetic operations. For example, a list of this week's 10 best- selling books does not convey by how much the #1 book outsold the #2 book. Discrete data and continuous data are types of nominal or quantitative data. These are data types on which arithmetic operations can be performed. For example, data on cash or unit sales for this week's 10 best-selling books allow us to make statements such as "the #1 book outsold the #2 book by 10%."

questions.question: A portfolio is equally invested in Stock A, with an expected return of 6%, and Stock B, with an expected return of 10%, and a risk-free asset with a return of 5%. The expected return on the portfolio is:
A bubble line chart is best suited to summarizing data with:
questions.explanation: A bubble line chart is a version of a line chart where data points are replaced with varying sized bubbles to represent a third dimension of the data (i.e., one not represented by either the vertical or horizontal axis).

questions.question: Which of the following statements regarding frequency distributions ideast accurate? Frequency distributions:

options.A: work with all types of measurement scales.
options.B: summarize data into a relatively small number of intervals.

questions.explanation: Data in a frequency distribution must belong to only one group or interval. Intervals are mutually exclusive and non-overlapping.

questions.question: GDP growth rates experienced by the UK over each of the last 10 years are most appropriately organized in:

options.C: both a one or two-dimensional array.

questions.explanation: A one-dimensional array is used when tabulating a single variable.

questions.question: An analyst calculates a winsorized mean return of 3.2% for an investment fund. This measure most likely.

options.A: replaces outliers with less extreme returns.
options.C: captures the compounded growth rate of the fund.

questions.explanation: The winsorized mean is a technique for dealing with outliers. For example, a 90% winsorized mean replaces the lowest 5% of values with the fifth percentile, and replaces the highest 5% of values with the 95th percentile. The arithmetic mean weights all observations equally. The geometric mean captures the compounded growth rate of the

questions.question: For the past three years, Acme Corp. has generated the following sample returns on equity (ROE): 4%, 10%, and 1%. What is the sample variance of the ROE over the last three years?
A distribution with a mode of 10 and a range of 2 to 25 wouldnost likelybe:
questions.explanation: The distance to the left from the mode to the beginning of the range is 8. The distance to the right from the mode to the end of the range is 15. Therefore, the distribution is skewed to the right, which means that it is positively skewed.

questions.question: A distribution that has positive excess kurtosis is:

options.A: more peaked than a normal distribution.
options.B: more skewed than a normal distribution.
options.C: less peaked than a normal distribution.

questions.explanation: A distribution with positive excess kurtosis is more peaked and has fatter tails than a normal distribution.

questions.question: The dividend yield of the S&P 500 index is least likely an example of:
questions.explanation: Nominal data are categorical data that cannot be ordered in a logical manner. An example would be industry classifications by GICS. The dividend yield of the S&P 500 is an example of numerical data as it represents measured or counted quantities. It is also an example of continuous data as the dividend yield can take on any numerical value within a range of specified values.

questions.question: Find the respective mean and the mean absolute deviation (MAD) of a series of stock market returns. Year 1 14% Year 2 20% Year 3 24% Year 4 22%
questions.explanation: (14 + 20 + 24 + 22) / 4 = 20 (mean) Take the absolute value of the differences and divide by n: MAD=[ 14-20 + 20-20 + 24-20 + 22 - 20 1/ 4 = 3%.

questions.question: Which of the following tools is most appropriate for visualizing how frequently certain words appear in a set of social media posts?
questions.explanation: A word cloud is a visual way of representing unstructured textual data. Each word is displayed in a size proportional to its frequency in the text.

questions.question: In a negatively skewed distribution, what is the order (from lowest value to highest) for the distribution's mode, mean, and median values?
questions.explanation: In a negatively skewed distribution, the mean is less than the median, which is less than the mode.

questions.question: Given the following set of data: 17, 3, 13, 3, 5, 9, 8 The value 8 is most accuratelydescribed as the:
questions.explanation: Median = middle of distribution = 8 (middle number); Mean = (3 + 3 + 5 + 8 + 9 + 13 + 17) / 7 = 8.28; Mode = most frequent observation = 3.

questions.question: Given the following frequency distribution: Frequency Return -10% up to 0% 5 0% up to 10% 7 10% up to 20% 9 20% up to 30% 6 30% up to 40% 3 What is the cumulative relative frequency of the 20% up to 30% return interval?
questions.explanation: Total number of observations = 30. Cumulative relative frequency = (5 + 7 + 9 + 6) / 30 = 90%

questions.question: Annual Returns on ABC Mutual Fund Yr 1 Yr 2 Yr 3 Yr 4 Yr 5 Yr 6 Yr 7 Yr 8 Yr 9 Yr 10 11.0% 12.5% 9.0% 13.0% 7.0% 15.0% 11.0% 8.0% 2.0% -16.5% Assuming a mean of 7.2%, what is the sample standard deviation of the returns for ABC Mutual Fund for the period from Year 1 to Year 10?
A distribution that is more peaked than a normal distribution is termed:
questions.explanation: A distribution that is more peaked than normal is leptokurtic. A leptokurtic distribution has fatter tails compared to a normal distribution. This means there is a greater chance of observing extreme outcomes. Market returns are leptokurtic. A distribution that is flatter than a normal distribution is termed platykurtic.

questions.question: Panel data are most accurately described as:

options.A: a type of time series data.
options.B: a combination of time series and cross-sectional data.

questions.explanation: Panel data combine time series and cross-sectional data into a single display, typically a table. For example, annual rates of return for the last 10 years (time series) can be shown for selected companies' common stocks (cross-section).

questions.question: Compute the standard deviation of a two-stock portfolio if stock A (40% weight) has a variance of 0.0015, stock B (60% weight) has a variance of 0.0021, and the correlation coefficient for the two stocks is -0.35?

John purchased 60% of the stocks in a portfolio, while Andrew purchased the other 40%. Half of John's stock-picks are considered good, while a fourth of Andrew's are considered to be good. If a randomly chosen stock is a good one, what is the probability John selected it?
questions.explanation: Using the information of the stock being good, the probability is updated to a conditional probability: P/ohn / good = P(good and John) / P(good). P(good and John) = P(good | John) × P(ohn) = 0.5 × 0.6 = 0.3. P(good and Andrew) = 0.25 × 0.40 = 0.10. P(good) = P(good and John) + P (good and Andrew) = 0.40. P/ohn / good) = P(good and John) / P(good) = 0.3 / 0.4 = 0.75.

questions.question: A supervisor is evaluating ten subordinates for their annual performance reviews. According to a new corporate policy, for every ten employees, two must be evaluated as "exceeds expectations," seven as "meets expectations," and one as "does not meet expectations." How many different ways is it possible for the supervisor to assign these ratings?
questions.explanation: The number of different ways to assign these labels is: 3,628,800 10! 2! × 7! x 1! = 2 x 5,040 x 1 - = 360

questions.question: The covariance of the returns on investments X and Y is 18.17. The standard deviation of returns on X is 7%, and the standard deviation of returns on Y is 4%. What is the value of the correlation coefficient for returns on investments X and Y?
questions.explanation: The correlation coefficient = Cov (X,Y) / [(Std Dev. X)(Std. Dev. Y)] = 18.17 / 28 = 0.65

questions.question: If the probability of an event is 0.20, what are the odds against the event occurring?
questions.explanation: The answer can be determined by dividing the probability of the event by the probability that it will not occur: (1/5) / (4/5) = 1 to 4. The odds against the event occurring is four to one, i.e. in five occurrences of the event, it is expected that it will occur once and not occur four times.

questions.question: The probability that interest rates will increase this year is 40%, and the probability that inflation will be over 2% is 30%. If inflation is over 2%, the probability of an increase in interest rates is 50%. The probability that inflation will be over 2% or interest rates increase this year is:
questions.explanation: Prob(interest rates increase) + Prob(inflation is over 2%) - Probinterest rates increase | inflation is over 2%) × Prob(inflation is over 2%) = 0.4 + 0.3 - 0.5 × 0.3 = 55%.

questions.question: The following table summarizes the results of a poll taken of CEO's and analysts concerning the economic impact of a pending piece of legislation: Think it will have a negative Think it will have a positive Group Total impact impact 40 CEO's 30 70 Analysts 70 60 130 200 110 90 What is the probability that a randomly selected individual from this group will be either an analyst or someone who thinks this legislation will have a positive impact on the economy?
questions.explanation: There are 130 total analysts and 40 CEOs who think it will have a positive impact. (130 + 40) / 200 = 0.85.

questions.question: A recent study indicates that the probability that a company's earnings will exceed consensus expectations equals 50%. From this analysis, the odds that the company's earnings exceed expectations are:
questions.explanation: Odds for an event equals the ratio of the probability of success to the probability of failure. If the probability of success is 50%, then there are equal probabilities of success and failure, and the odds for success are 1 to 1.

questions.question: The returns on assets C and Dare strongly correlated with a correlation coefficient of 0.80. The variance of returns on C is 0.0009, and the variance of returns on D is 0.0036. What is the covariance of returns on C and D?
A very large company has equal amounts of male and female employees. If a random sample of four employees is selected, what is the probability that all four employees selected are female?
questions.explanation: Each employee has equal chance of being male or female. Hence, probability of selecting four female employees = (0.5f = 0.0625

questions.question: If the probability of an event is 0.10, what are the odds for the event occurring?
questions.explanation: The answer can be determined by dividing the probability of the event by the probability that it will not occur: (1/10) / (9/10) = 1 to 9. The probability of the event occurring is one to nine, i.e. in ten occurrences of the event, it is expected that it will occur once and not occur nine times.

questions.question: A company has two machines that produce widgets. An older machine produces 16% defective widgets, while the new machine produces only 8% defective widgets. In addition, the new machine employs a superior production process such that it produces three times as many widgets as the older machine does. Given that a widget was produced by the new machine, what is the probability it is NOT defective?
questions.explanation: The problem is just asking for the conditional probability of a defective widget given that it was produced by the new machine. Since the widget was produced by the new machine and not selected from the output randomly (if randomly selected, you would not know which machine produced the widget), we know there is an 8% chance it is defective. Hence, the probability it is not defective is the complement, 1 - 8% = 92%.

questions.question: There is a 60% chance that the economy will be good next year and a 40% chance that it will be bad. If the economy is good, there is a 70% chance that XYZ Incorporated will have EPS of $5.00 and a 30% chance that their earnings will be $3.50. If the economy is bad, there is an 80% chance that XYZ Incorporated will have EPS of $1.50 and a 20% chance that their earnings will be $1.00. What is the firm's expected EPS?
questions.explanation: Joint Joint Conditional State of the Economy Probability EPS Probability (Unconditional Probability) Probability × EPS 60% × 70% 42% × $5.00 $5.00 70% = 42% = $2.10 GOOD 60% 60% × 30% 18% × $3.50 30% $3.50 = $0.63 = 18% 32% × $1.50 40% × 80% 80% $1.50 = 32% = $0.48 BAD 40% 40% × 20% 8% × $1.00 = 20% $1.00 $0.08 = 8% $3.29 Expected EPS = ≤ Joint Probability × EPS

questions.question: Which probability rule determines the probability that two events will both occur?
questions.explanation: The multiplication rule is used to determine the joint probability of two events. The addition rule is used to determine the probability that at least one of two events will occur. The total probability rule is utilized when trying to determine the unconditional probability of an event.

questions.question: If the outcome of event A is not affected by event B, then events A and B are said to be:
questions.explanation: If the outcome of one event does not influence the outcome of another, then the events are independent.

questions.question: Marc Chausset, CFA, will be assigning ratings of either outperform, market perform, or underperform to the 12 stocks he follows. If he assigns each rating to the same number of stocks, the number of ways he can do this ismost appropriatelydetermined using:
questions.explanation: This particular counting problem is a labeling problem. There are 12! / (4! × 4! × 4!) = 34,650 ways to label four stocksoutperform, four stocks market perform, and four stocks underperform. Neither the permutation formula nor the combination formula is appropriate for solving this counting problem.

questions.question: For assets A and B we know the following: E(R) = 0.10, E(R) = 0.20, Var(RA) = 0.25, Var(R) = 0.36 and the correlation of the returns is 0.6. What is the expected return of a portfolio that is equally invested in the two assets?
questions.explanation: The expected return of a portfolio composed of n-assets is the weighted average of the expected returns of the assets in the portfolio: ((W)x (E(R)) + ((W2)x (E(R)) = (0.5 x 0.1) + (0.5 × 0.2) = 0.15.

questions.question: Helen Pedersen has all her money invested in either of two mutual funds (Y and Z). She knows that there is a 40% probability that Fund Y will rise in price and a 60% probability that Fund Z will rise in price if Fund Y rises in price. What is the probability that both Fund Y and Fund Z will rise in price?
questions.explanation: Here we are calculating a joint probability. We know there is a 40% chance that Y rises and a 60% chance the Z rises if Y also rises (conditional probability). To find the probability that both rise, we simply multiply these probabilities together. P(Y) = 0.40, P(Z | Y) = 0.60. Therefore, P(YZ) = P(Y)P(Z | Y) = 0.40(0.60) = 0.24.

questions.question: Firm A can fall short, meet, or exceed its earnings forecast. Each of these events is equally likely. Whether firm A increases its dividend will depend upon these outcomes. Respectively, the probabilities of a dividend increase conditional on the firm falling short, meeting or exceeding the forecast are 20%, 30%, and 50%. The unconditional probability of a dividend increase is:
questions.explanation: The unconditional probability is the weighted average of the conditional probabilities where the weights are the probabilities of the conditions. In this problem the three conditions fall short, meet, or exceed its earnings forecast are all equally likely. Therefore, the unconditional probability is the simple average of the three conditional probabilities: (0.2 + 0.3 + 0.5) ÷ 3.

questions.question: Which of the following statements about probability isnost accurate?

options.A: An outcome is the calculated probability of an event. A conditional probability is the probability that two or more events will happen concurrently.
options.C: An event is a set of one or more possible values of a random variable.

questions.explanation: Conditional probability is the probability of one event happening given that another event has happened. An outcome is the numerical result associated with a random variable.

Each lottery ticket discloses the odds of winning. These odds are based on:

options.B: the best estimate of the Department of Gaming.

questions.explanation: An a priori probability is based on formal reasoning rather than on historical results or subjective opinion.

questions.question: An investor has two stocks, Stock R and Stock S in her portfolio. Given the following information on the two stocks, the portfolio's standard deviation is/osestto: • OR = 34% • Os = 16% • rR,S = 0.67 • WR = 80% • Ws = 20%
The following table shows the weightings and expected returns for a portfolio of three stocks: Stock Weight E(R) V 0.40 12% 8% 0.35 S 0.25 5% What is the expected return of this portfolio?
questions.explanation: The expected return is simply a weighted average return. Multiplying the weight of each asset by its expected return, then summing, produces: E(RP) = 0.40(12) + 0.35(8) + 0.25(5) = 8.85%. State of the Economy Weight E(Rx) Probability × Return 0.4 × 12% V 0.40 12% M 0.35 × 8% 0.35 8% S 0.25 × 5% 0.25 5% Expected Return = [ Weight × E(Rx) 8.85%

questions.question: The following table summarizes the availability of trucks with air bags and bucket seats at a dealership. Bucket Seats No Bucket Seats Total Air Bags 125 75 50 No Air Bags 35 60 95 110 110 220 Total What is the probability of selecting a truck at random that has either air bags or bucket seats?
questions.explanation: The addition rule for probabilities is used to determine the probability of at least one event among two or more events occurring. The probability of each event is added and the joint probability (if the events are not mutually exclusive) is subtracted to arrive at the solution. P(air bags or bucket seats) = P(air bags) + P(bucket seats) - P(air bags and bucket seats) = (125 / 220) + (110 / 220) - (75 / 220) = 0.57 + 0.50 - 0.34 = 0.73 or 73%. Alternative: 1 - P(no airbag and no bucket seats) = 1 - (60 / 220) = 72.7%

questions.question: A conditional expectation involves:

options.A: refining a forecast because of the occurrence of some other event.

questions.explanation: Conditional expected values are contingent upon the occurrence of some other event. The expectation changes as new information is revealed.

questions.question: There is a 50% probability that the Fed will cut interest rates tomorrow. On any given day, there is a 67% probability the DJIA will increase. On days the Fed cuts interest rates, the probability the DJIA will go up is 90%. What is the probability that tomorrow the Fed will cut interest rates or the DJIA will go up?
questions.explanation: This requires the addition formula. From the information: P(cut interest rates) = 0.50 and P(DJIA increase) = 0.67, P(DJIA increase | cut interest rates) = 0.90. The joint probability is 0.50 × 0.90 = 0.45. Thus P (cut interest rates or DJIA increase) = 0.50 + 0.67 - 0.45 = 0.72.

questions.question: A two-sided but very thick coin is expected to land on its edge twice out of every 100 flips. And the probability of face up (heads) and the probability of face down (tails) are equal. When the coin is flipped, the prize is $1 for heads, $2 for tails, and $50 when the coin lands on its edge. What is the expected value of the prize on a single coin toss?
questions.explanation: We need to calculate of probability weighted average payoff. Since the probability of the coin landing on its edge is 0.02, the probability of each of the other two events is 0.49. The expected payoff is: (0.02 x $50) + (0.49 x $1) + (0.49 x $2) = $2.47. Outcome Probability Payoff Probability × Payoff 2 / 100 = 2% 2% × $50 Edge $50 $1 Heads 49% × $1 49% $2 49% × $2 Tails 49% Expected Payoff = { Probability × Payoff $2.47

questions.question: Tully Advisers, Inc., has determined four possible economic scenarios and has projected the portfolio returns for two portfolios for their client under each scenario. Tully's economist has estimated the probability of each scenario as shown in the table below. Given this information, what is the expected return on Portfolio A? Scenario Probability Return on Portfolio A Return on Portfolio B 19% 17% A 15% 20% 18% 14% B 25% 12% 10% C 40% 8% 9% D
questions.explanation: The expected return on Portfolio A is a probability-weighted average of 17%, 14%, 12%, and 8%. Expected return = (0.15)(0.17) + (0.20)(0.14) + (0.25)(0.12) + (0.40)(0.08) = 0.1155 or 11.55%. Scenario Probability Return on Portfolio A Portfolio × Weight 15% 15 × 17% A 17% 20% 20% × 14% B 14% 25% 12% 25% × 12% 40% × 8% D 40% 8% Probability Weighted Average Return 11.55% [ Probability × Weight

questions.question: The probabilities that the prices of shares of Alpha Publishing and Omega Software will fall below $35 in the next six months are 65% and 47%. If these probabilities are independent, the probability that the shares of at least one of the companies will fall below $35 in the next six months is:
questions.explanation: We calculate the probability that at least one of the options will fall below $35 using the addition rule for probabilities (A represents Alpha, O represents Omega): P(A or O) = P(A) + P(O) - P(A and O), where P(A and O) = P(A) x P(O) P(A or O) = 0.65 + 0.47 - (0.65 × 0.47) = approximately 0.81

questions.question: An analyst expects that 20% of all publicly traded companies will experience a decline in earnings next year. The analyst has developed a ratio to help forecast this decline. If the company has a decline in earnings, there is a 90% probability that this ratio will be negative. If the company does not have a decline in earnings, there is only a 10% probability that the ratio will be negative. The analyst randomly selects a company with a negative ratio. Based on Bayes' theorem, the updated probability that the company will experience a decline is:
questions.explanation: Given a set of prior probabilities for an event of interest, Bayes' formula is used to update the probability of the event, in this case that the company we have already selected will experience a decline in earnings next year. Bayes' formula says to divide the Probability of New Information given Event by the Unconditional Probability of New Information and multiply that result by the Prior Probability of the Event. In this case, P(company having a decline in earnings next year) = 0.20 is divided by 0.26 (which is the Unconditional Probability that a company having an earnings decline will have a negative ratio (90% have negative ratios of the 20% which have earnings declines) plus (10% have negative ratios of the 80% which do not have earnings declines) or ((0.90) × (0.20)) + ((0.10) × (0.80)) = 0.26.) This result is then multiplied by the Prior Probability of the ratio being negative, 0.90. The result is (0.20 / 0.26) × (0.90) = 0.69 or 69%.

questions.question: An investor is considering purchasing ACQ. There is a 30% probability that ACQ will be acquired in the next two months. If ACQ is acquired, there is a 40% probability of earning a 30% return on the investment and a 60% probability of earning 25%. If ACQ is not acquired, the expected return is 12%. What is the expected return on this investment?

A bond portfolio consists of four BB-rated bonds. Each has a probability of default of 24% and these probabilities are independent. What are the probabilities of all the bonds defaulting and the probability of all the bonds not defaulting, respectively?
questions.explanation: For the four independent events where the probability is the same for each, the probability of all defaulting is (0.24). The probability of all not defaulting is (1 - 0.24).

questions.question: A firm wants to select a team of five from a group of ten employees. How many ways can the firm compose the team of five?
questions.explanation: This is a labeling problem where there are only two labels: chosen and not chosen. Thus, the combination formula applies: 10! / (5! × 5!) = 3,628,800 / (120 × 120) = 252. With a TI calculator: 10 [2nd][nCr] 5 = 252.

questions.question: The probability that tomorrow's high temperature will be below 32 degrees F is 20%. The probability that tomorrow's high temperature will be above 40 degrees F is 10%. These two events are:
questions.explanation: If two events cannot occur simultaneously, the events are mutually exclusive. The high temperature tomorrow cannot beboth below 32 and above 40. If two events are independent, the occurrence of one event does not affect the probability of occurrence of the other. Because these events are mutually exclusive, they cannot be independent; if one of them occurs, the probability of the other is zero. For two events to be exhaustive, they must encompass the entire range of possible outcomes (that is, their probabilities sum to 100%). Here this is not the case as there are possible outcomes where the high temperature is between 32 and 40.

questions.question: Let A and B be two mutually exclusive events with P(A) = 0.40 and P(B) = 0.20. Therefore:
questions.explanation: If the two evens are mutually exclusive, the probability of both occurring is zero.

questions.question: The probability of rolling a 3 on the fourth roll of a fair 6-sided die: 8

options.A: depends on the results of the three previous rolls.
options.C: is equal to the probability of rolling a 3 on the first roll.

questions.explanation: Because each event is independent, the probability does not change for each roll. For a six-sided die the probability of rolling a 3 (or any other number from 1 to 6) on a single rol is 1/6.

questions.question: The following information is available concerning expected return and standard deviation of Pluto and Neptune Corporations: Expected Return Standard Deviation Pluto Corporation 11% 0.22 0.13 Neptune Corporation 9% If the correlation between Pluto and Neptune is 0.25, determine the expected return and standard deviation of a portfolio that consists of 65% Pluto Corporation stock and 35% Neptune Corporation stock.
The probabilities of earning a specified return from a portfolio are shown below: Return Probability 0.20 10% 0.20 20% 22% 0.20 15% 0.20 0.20 25% What are the odds of earning at least 20%?
questions.explanation: Odds are the number of successful possibilities to the number of unsuccessful possibilities: P(E)/[1 - P(E)] or 0.6 / 0.4 or 3/2.

questions.question: The covariance of returns on two investments over a 10-year period is 0.009. If the variance of returns for investment A is 0.020 and the variance of returns for investment B is 0.033, what is the correlation coefficient for the returns?
questions.explanation: The correlation coefficient is: Cov(A,B) / [(Std Dev A)(Std Dev B)] = 0.009 / [0.02)(V0.033)] = 0.350

questions.question: An economist estimates a 60% probability that the economy will expand next year. The technology sector has a 70% probability of outperforming the market if the economy expands and a 10% probability of outperforming the market if the economy does not expand. Given the new information that the technology sector will not outperform the market, the probability that the economy will not expand islosestto:
questions.explanation: 70% Technology Does Outperform 42% Expands and Outperforms 60% Economy Does Expand 30% Technology Does Not Outperform 3% Expands a des Not Outperfo 10% Technology Does Outperform 6 Does Not Expa id Outperforr 40% Economy Does Not Expand 90% Technology Does Not Outperform 36% Does Not Expand and Does Not Outperform Using the new information we can use Bayes' formula to update the probability. P(economy does not expand | tech does not outperform) = P(economy does not expand and tech does not outperform) / P(tech does not outperform). P(economy does not expand and tech does not outperform) = P(tech does not outperform | economy does not expand) × P(economy does not expand) = 0.90 × 0.40 = 0.36. Pleconomy does expand and tech does not outperform) = P(tech does not outperform | economy does expand) × P(economy does expand) = 0.30 × 0.60 = 0.18. P(economy does not expand) = 1.00 - P(economy does expand) = 1.00 - 0.60 = 0.40. P(tech does not outperform | economy does not expand) = 1.00 - P(tech does outperform economy does not expand) = 1.00 - 0.10 = 0.90. P(tech does not outperform) = P(tech does not outperform and economy does not expand) + P(tech does not outperform and economy does expand) = 0.36 + 0.18 = 0.54. P(economy does not expand | tech does not outperform) = P(economy does not expand and tech does not outperform) / P(tech does not outperform) = 0.36 / 0.54 = 0.67.

questions.question: Thomas Baynes has applied to both Harvard and Yale. Baynes has determined that the probability of getting into Harvard is 25% and the probability of getting into Yale (his father's alma mater) is 42%. Baynes has also determined that the probability of being accepted at both schools is 2.8%. What is the probability of Baynes being accepted at either Harvard or Yale?
questions.explanation: Using the addition rule, the probability of being accepted at Harvard or Yale is equal to: P(Harvard) + P(Yale) - P(Harvard and Yale) = 0.25 + 0.42 - 0.028 = 0.642 or 64.2%.

questions.question: Assume two stocks are perfectly negatively correlated. Stock A has a standard deviation of 10.2% and stock B has a standard deviation of 13.9%. What is the standard deviation of the portfolio if 75% is invested in A and 25% in B?

If the probability of both a new Wal-Mart and a new Wendy's being built next month is 68% and the probability of a new Wal-Mart being built is 85%, what is the probability of a new Wendy's being built if a new Wal-Mart is built?

A firm holds two $50 million bonds with call dates this week. • The probability that Bond A will be called is 0.80. • The probability that Bond B will be called is 0.30. The probability that at least one of the bonds will be called islosest to.
questions.explanation: We calculate the probability that at least one of the bonds will be called using the addition rule for probabilities: P(A or B) = P(A) + P(B) - P(A and B), where P(A and B) = P(A) × P(B) P(A or B) = 0.80 + 0.30 - (0.8 × 0.3) = 0.86

questions.question: Pat Binder, CFA, is examining the effect of an inverted yield curve on the stock market. She determines that in the past century, when the yield curve has inverted, a bear market ensued 75% of the time. Binder believes the probability of an inverted yield curve in the next year is 20%. The probability that there will be an inverted yield curve next year followed by a bear market is closest to:
questions.explanation: This is a joint probability. From the information: P(Bear Market given inverted yield curve) = 0.75 and P(inverted yield curve) = 0.20. The joint probability is the product of these two probabilities: (0.75)(0.20) = 0.15.

questions.question: An empirical probability is one that is:
questions.explanation: An empirical probability is one that is derived from analyzing past data. For example, a basketball player has scored at least 22 points in each of the season's 18 games. Therefore, there is a high probability that he will score 22 points in tonight's game.

questions.question: A parking lot has 100 red and blue cars in it. • 40% of the cars are red. • 70% of the red cars have radios. • 80% of the blue cars have radios. What is the probability of selecting a car at random and having it be red and have a radio?
questions.explanation: Here we are calculating a joint probability. In this case, it's that a car selected is rednd has a radio. We need to multiply the unconditional probability of selecting a red car by the conditional probability of selecting a car with a radiogiven that it is a red car: P(red and radio) = (P(red)) x (P(radio/red)) = (0.4) × (0.7) = 0.28 or 28%. Unconditional Probability Conditional Probability Joint Probability (A) (B) A × B Radio 70% 28% (Red + Radio) 40% Red No Radio 30% 12%(Red + NO Radio) Radio 80% 48% (Blue + Radio) 60% Blue No Radio 20% 12% (Blue + NO Radio) Total 100% 100%
