questions.explanation: The level of significance is the probability of rejecting the null hypothesis when it is true. The probability of rejecting the null when it is false is the power of a test. (Module 6.1, LOS

questions.question: George Appleton believes that the average return on equity in the amusement industry, M, is greater than 10%. What is the null (H) and alternative (Ha) hypothesis for his study?
questions.explanation: The alternative hypothesis is determined by the theory or the belief. The researcher specifies the null as the hypothesis that he wishes to reject (in favor of the alternative). Note that this is a one-sided alternative because of the "greater than" belief.

questions.question: A researcher is testing whether the average age of employees in a large firm is statistically different from 35 years (either above or below). A sample is drawn of 250 employees and the researcher determines that the appropriate critical value for the test statistic is 1.96. The value of the computed test statistic is 4.35. Given this information, which of the following statements is least accurate? The test:

options.B: indicates that the researcher will reject the null hypothesis. indicates that the researcher is 95% confident that the average employee age i different than 35 years.

questions.explanation: This test has a significance level of 5%.The relationship between confidence and significance is: significance level = 1 - confidence level. We know that the significance level is 5% because the sample size is large and the critical value of the test statistic is 1.96 (2.5% of probability is in both the upper and lower tails).

questions.question: For a test of the equality of the mean returns of two non-independent populations based on a sample, the numerator of the appropriate test statistic is the:

options.A: average difference between pairs of returns.
options.B: difference between the sample means for each population.
options.C: larger of the two sample means.

questions.explanation: A hypothesis test of the equality of the means of two normally distributed non- independent populations (hypothesized mean difference = 0) is a t-test and the numerator is the average difference between the sample returns over the sample period.

questions.question: Which of the following statements/east accuratelydescribes the procedure for testing a hypothesis?

options.A: Develop a hypothesis, compute the test statistic, and make a decision.
options.B: Select the level of significance, formulate the decision rule, and make a decisio x Compute the sample value of the test statistic, set up a rejection (critical) regio and make a decision.

questions.explanation: Depending upon the author there can be as many as seven steps in hypothesis testing which are: 1. Stating the hypotheses. 2. Identifying the test statistic and its probability distribution. 3. Specifying the significance level. 4. Stating the decision rule. 5. Collecting the data and performing the calculations. 6. Making the statistical decision. 7. Making the economic or investment decision.

questions.question: Student's t-Distribution Level of Significance for One-Tailed Test df 0.100 0.050 0.025 0.01 0.005 0.0005 Level of Significance for Two-Tailed Test 0.01 0.001 0.20 0.10 0.02 df 0.05 3.551 40 1.303| 1.684 2.021 2.423 2.704 Ken Wallace is interested in testing whether the average price to earnings (P/E) of firms in the retail industry is 25. Using at-distributed test statistic and a 5% level of significance, the critical values for a sample of 41 firms is (are):

questions.question: In order to test whether the mean IQ of employees in an organization is greater than 100, a sample of 30 employees is taken and the sample value of the computed test statisticnty = 3.4. The null and alternative hypotheses are:
questions.explanation: The null hypothesis is that the population mean is less than or equal to from 100. The alternative hypothesis is that the population mean is greater than 100.

questions.question: Ron Jacobi, manager with the Toulee Department of Natural Resources, is responsible for setting catch-and-release limits for Lake Norby, a large and popular fishing lake. He takes a sample to determine whether the mean length of Northern Pike in the lake exceeds 18 inches. If the sample t-statistic indicates that the mean length of the fish is significantly greater than 18 inches, when the population mean is actually 17.8 inches, the t-test resulted in:

options.A: both a Type I and a Type II error.

questions.explanation: Rejection of a null hypothesis when it is actually true is a Type I error. Here, Hu ≤ 18 inches and Ha: u > 18 inches. Type Il error is failing to reject a null hypothesis when it is actually false. Because a Type I error can only occur if the null hypothesis is true, and a Type Il error can only occur if the null hypothesis is false, it is logically impossible for a test to result in both types of error at the same time.

questions.question: A survey is taken to determine whether the average starting salaries of CFA charterholders is equal to or greater than $59,000 per year. What is the test statistic given a sample of 135 newly acquired CFA charterholders with a mean starting salary of $64,000 and a standard deviation of $5,500?
questions.explanation: With a large sample size (135) thez-statistic is used. Thez-statistic is calculated by subtracting the hypothesized parameter from the parameter that has been estimated and dividing the difference by the standard error of the sample statistic. Here, the test statistic = (sample mean - hypothesized mean) / (population standard deviation / (sample sizef2) = (X - 4) / (o / n'/2) = (64,000 - 59,000) / (5,500 / 135/2) = (5,000) / (5,500 / 11.62) = 10.56.

questions.question: The variance of 100 daily stock returns for Stock A is 0.0078. The variance of 90 daily stock returns for Stock B is 0.0083. Using a 5% level of significance, the critical value for this test is 1.61. The most appropriateconclusion regarding whether the variance of Stock A is different from the variance of Stock B is that the:

options.A: variance of Stock B is significantly greater than the variance of Stock A.

questions.explanation: A test of the equality of variances requires an F-statistic. The calculated F-statistic is 0.0083/0.0078 = 1.064. Since the calculated F value of 1.064 is less than the critical F value of 1.61, we cannot reject the null hypothesis that the variances of the 2 stocks are equal.

questions.question: Which of the following statements regarding hypothesis testing iseast accurate?

options.A: A type I error is acceptance of a hypothesis that is actually false.
options.B: The significance level is the risk of making a type I error.
options.C: A type Il error is the acceptance of a hypothesis that is actually false.

questions.explanation: A type l error is the rejection of a hypothesis that is actually true.

questions.question: The power of the test is:

options.A: the probability of rejecting a false null hypothesis.
options.B: equal to the level of confidence.
options.C: the probability of rejecting a true null hypothesis.

questions.explanation: This is the definition of the power of the test: the probability of correctly rejecting the null hypothesis (rejecting the null hypothesis when it is false).

questions.question: A Type I error is made when the researcher:

options.A: rejects the null hypothesis when it is actually true.
options.B: rejects the alternative hypothesis when it is actually true.
options.C: fails to reject the null hypothesis when it is actually false.

questions.explanation: A Type I error is defined as rejecting the null hypothesis when it is actually true. It can be thought of as a false positive. A Type II error occurs when a researching fails to reject the null hypothesis when it is false. It can be thought of as a false negative.

questions.question: Which of the following statements about hypothesis testing isnost accurate? A Type I error is the probability of:

options.C: failing to reject a false hypothesis.

questions.explanation: The Type I error is the error of rejecting the null hypothesis when, in fact, the null is true.

questions.question: If a two-tailed hypothesis test has a 5% probability of rejecting the null hypothesis when the null is true, it is most likelythat the:

options.A: probability of a Type I error is 2.5%.
options.C: significance level of the test is 5%.

questions.explanation: Rejecting the null hypothesis when it is true is a Type I error. The probability of a Type I error is the significance level of the test. The power of a test is one minus the probability of a Type Il error, which cannot be calculated from the information given.

questions.question: Which of the following statements about hypothesis testing isnost accurate? A Type II error is the probability of:

options.A: failing to reject a false null hypothesis.

questions.explanation: The Type Il error is the error of failing to reject a null hypothesis that is not true.

questions.question: Brian Ci believes that the average return on equity in the airline industry, u, is less than 5%. What are the appropriate null () and alternative (Ha) hypotheses to test this belief?
questions.explanation: The null must be either equal to, less than or equal to, or greater than or equal to.

questions.question: An analyst plans to use the following test statistic: X-MO tn-1 = s/VI This test statistic is appropriate for a hypothesis about:

options.A: the population mean of a normal distribution with unknown variance.
options.B: the mean difference of two normal populations. the equality of two population means of two normally distributed populations based on independent samples.

questions.explanation: When testing hypotheses about the population mean, the sample standard deviation must be used in the denominator of the test statistic when the population standard deviation is unknown, the population is normal, and/or the sample is large. The statistic is astat with n - 1 degrees of freedom. The numerator is the sampling error for the population mean if the true mean is u0 and the denominator is the standard error of the sample mean

questions.question: Jo Su believes that there should be a negative relation between returns and systematic risk. She intends to collect data on returns and systematic risk to test this theory. What is the appropriate alternative hypothesis?
questions.explanation: The alternative hypothesis is determined by the theory or the belief. The researcher specifies the null as the hypothesis that she wishes to reject (in favor of the alternative). The theory in this case is that the correlation is negative.

questions.question: A Type I error:

options.B: fails to reject a false null hypothesis.

questions.explanation: A Type | Error is defined as rejecting the null hypothesis when it is actually true. The probability of committing a Type I error is the significance level or alpha risk.

questions.question: Of the following explanations, which isleast likelyto be a valid explanation for divergence between statistical significance and economic significance?
questions.explanation: While data errors would certainly come to bear on the analysis, in their presence we would not be able to assert either statistical or economic significance. In other words, data errors are not a valid explanation. The others are factors that can produce statistically significant results that are not economically significant.

questions.question: Robert Patterson, an options trader, believes that the return on options trading is higher on Mondays than on other days. In order to test his theory, he formulates a null hypothesis. Which of the following would be an appropriate null hypothesis? Returns on Mondays are:

options.A: not greater than returns on other days.
options.B: greater than returns on other days.
options.C: less than returns on other days.

questions.explanation: An appropriate null hypothesis is one that the researcher wants to reject. If Patterson believes that the returns on Mondays are greater than on other days, he would like to reject the hypothesis that the opposite is true-that returns on Mondays are not greater than returns on other days.

questions.question: To test a hypothesis that the population correlation coefficient of two variables is equal to zero, an analyst collects a sample of 24 observations and calculates a sample correlation coefficient of 0.37. Can the analyst test this hypothesis using only these two inputs?

options.B: No, because the sample means of the two variables are also required. No, because the sample standard deviations of the two variables are also required.

questions.explanation: rVn-2 The t-statistic for a test of the population correlation coefficient is VI-ph, where ris the sample correlation coefficient and is the sample size.

questions.question: A researcher wants to test whether the weekly returns on two stocks are correlated. The test statistic for the appropriate test follows a:

options.B: t-distribution with n - 1 degrees of freedom.
options.C: t-distribution with n - 2 degrees of freedom.

questions.explanation: The test statistic for the significance of the correlation between two random variables

questions.question: A p-value of 0.02% means that a researcher:

options.A: can reject the null hypothesis at both the 5% and 1% significance levels. can reject the null hypothesis at the 5% significance level but cannot reject at
options.C: cannot reject the null hypothesis at either the 5% or 1% significance levels.

questions.explanation: A p-value of 0.02% means that the smallest significance level at which the hypothesis can be rejected is 0.0002, which is smaller than 0.05 or 0.01. Therefore the null hypothesis can be rejected at both the 5% and 1% significance levels.

questions.question: If the null hypothesis is innocence, then the statement "It is better that the guilty go free, than the innocent are punished" is an example of preferring a:

options.B: Type I error over a Type lI error.
options.C: Type Il error over a Type I error.

questions.explanation: The statement shows a preference for failing to reject the null hypothesis when it is false (a Type II error), over rejecting it when it is true (a Type I error).

questions.question: For a hypothesis test regarding a population parameter, an analyst has determined that the probability of failing to reject a false null hypothesis is 18%, and the probability of rejecting a true null hypothesis is 5%. The power of the test is:
questions.explanation: The power of the test is 1 - the probability of failing to reject a false null (Type Il error); 1 - 0.18 = 0.82.

questions.question: A researcher has random samples from two populations that are approximately normally distributed. He uses the ratio of the larger sample variance to the smaller sample variance to test the hypothesis that the true variances of the two populations are equal. The test statistic he has calculated will have what type of distribution?
questions.explanation: The test statistic for this test of the equality of variances follows an F-distribution.

questions.question: An analyst decides to select 10 stocks for her portfolio by placing the ticker symbols for all the stocks traded on the New York Stock Exchange in a large bowl. She randomly selects 20 stocks and will put every other one chosen into her 10-stock portfolio. The analyst used:
questions.explanation: In simple random sampling, each item in the population has an equal chance of being selected. The analyst's method meets this criterion.

questions.question: Which of the following statements about hypothesis testing isnost accurate?

options.A: The power of a test is one minus the probability of a Type I error. If you can disprove the null hypothesis, then you have proven the alternative hypothesis.
options.C: The probability of a Type I error is equal to the significance level of the test.

questions.explanation: The probability of getting a test statistic outside the critical value(s) when the null is true is the level of significance and is the probability of a Type I error. The power of a test is 1 minus the probability of a Type Il error. Hypothesis testing does not prove a hypothesis, we either reject the null or fail to reject it.

questions.question: An analyst has calculated the sample variances for two random samples from independent normally distributed populations. The test statistic for the hypothesis that the true population variances are equal is a(n):
questions.explanation: The ratio of the two sample variances follows an F distribution.

questions.question: A test of whether the population variance is equal to a hypothesized value requires the use of a test statistic that is:
questions.explanation: In tests of whether the variance of a population equals a particular value, the chi-squared test statistic is appropriate.

questions.question: Brandee Shoffield is the public relations manager for Night Train Express, a local sports team. Shoffield is trying to sell advertising spots and wants to know if she can say with 90% confidence that average home game attendance is greater than 3,000. Attendance is approximately normally distributed. A sample of the attendance at 15 home games results in a mean of 3,150 and a standard deviation of 450. Which of the following statements is most accurate? With an unknown population variance and a small sample size, no statistic is

questions.explanation: Here, we have a normally distributed population with an unknown variance (we are given only the sample standard deviation) and a small sample size (less than 30.) Thus, we will use the t-statistic. The test statistic = t= (3,150 - 3,000) / (450 / \ 15) = 1.291

questions.question: A manager wants to test whether two normally distributed and independent populations have equal variances. The appropriate test statistic for this test is a:
questions.explanation: For a test of the equality of two variances, the appropriate test statistic test is the F- statistic.

questions.question: Which of the following statements about hypothesis testing iseast accurate?

options.A: If the alternative hypothesis is Ha: u > Ho, a two-tailed test is appropriate.
options.B: The null hypothesis is a statement about the value of a population parameter. *
options.C: A Type Il error is failing to reject a false null hypothesis.

questions.explanation: The hypotheses are always stated in terms of a population parameter. Type I and Type II are the two types of errors you can make - reject a null hypothesis that is true or fail to reject a null hypothesis that is false. The alternative may be one-sided (in which case a > or < sign is used) or two-sided (in which case af is used).

questions.question: A survey is taken to determine whether the average starting salaries of CFA charterholders is equal to or greater than $62,500 per year. What is the test statistic given a sample of 125 newly acquired CFA charterholders with a mean starting salary of $65,000 and a standard deviation of $2,600?
questions.explanation: With a large sample size (125) and an unknown population variance, either the statistic or the z-statistic could be used. Using thez-statistic, it is calculated by subtracting the hypothesized parameter from the parameter that has been estimated and dividing the difference by the standard error of the sample statistic. The test statistic = (sample mean - hypothesized mean) / (sample standard deviation / (sample size?)) = (X - u) / (s / n'/2) = (65,000 - 62,500) / (2,600 / 125 2) = (2,500) / (2,600 / 11.18) = 10.75.

questions.question: A hypothesis test has ap-value of 1.96%. An analyst should reject the null hypothesis at a significance level of:

options.A: 6%, but not at a significance level of 4%.
options.B: 4%, but not at a significance level of 2%.
options.C: 3%, but not at a significance level of 1%.

questions.explanation: The p-value of 1.96% is the smallest level of significance at which the hypothesis can be rejected.

questions.question: Jill Woodall believes that the average return on equity in the retail industry, u, is less than 15%. What are the null (H) and alternative (Ha) hypotheses for her study?
questions.explanation: This is a one-sided alternative because of the "less than" belief.

questions.question: For a two-tailed test of hypothesis involving a z-distributed test statistic and a 5% level of significance, a calculated z-statistic of 1.5 indicates that:

options.C: the null hypothesis cannot be rejected.

questions.explanation: For a two-tailed test at a 5% level of significance the calculated z-statistic would have to be greater than the critical z value of 1.96 for the null hypothesis to be rejected.

questions.question: The test of the equality of the variances of two normally distributed populations requires the use of a test statistic that is:
questions.explanation: The F-distributed test statistic, F = s? / s22, is used to compare the variances of two populations.

questions.question: Joe Bay, CFA, wants to test the hypothesis that the variance of returns on energy stocks is equal to the variance of returns on transportation stocks. Bay assumes the samples are independent and the returns are normally distributed. The appropriate test statistic for this hypothesis is:
questions.explanation: Bay is testing a hypothesis about the equality of variances of two normally distributed populations. The test statistic used to test this hypothesis is an F-statistic. A chi-square statistic is used to test a hypothesis about the variance of a single population. A t-statistic is used to test hypotheses concerning a population mean, the differences between means of two populations, or the mean of differences between paired observations from two populations.

questions.question: A test of the hypothesis that two categorical variables are independent isnost likelyto employ:
questions.explanation: A hypothesis test whether of two categorical variables (e.g., company sector and bond rating) are independent can be performed by constructing a contingency table and calculating a chi-squared statistic.

questions.question: When there is a linear relationship between an independent variable and the relative change in the dependent variable, themost appropriatemodel for a simple regression is:
questions.explanation: A regression of the form In Y = b + biX is appropriate when the relative change in the dependent variable is a linear function of the independent variable.

questions.question: Which of the following isleast likely an assumption of linear regression?

options.A: Values of the independent variable are not correlated with the error term.
options.B: The error terms from a regression are positively correlated.
options.C: The variance of the error terms each period remains the same.

questions.explanation: One assumption of linear regression is that the error terms are independently distributed. In this case, the correlations between error terms are expected to be zero. Constant variance of the error terms and no correlation between the independent variable and the error term are assumptions of linear regression.

questions.question: In a simple regression model, the least squares criterion is to minimize the sum of squared differences between:

options.A: the estimated and actual slope coefficient.
options.B: the predicted and actual values of the dependent variable.
options.C: the intercept term and the residual term.

questions.explanation: The least squares criterion defines the best-fitting linear relationship as the one that minimizes the sum of squared errors, the squared vertical distances between the predicted and actual values of the dependent variable.

questions.question: Given the relationship: Y = 2.83 + 1.5X What is the predicted value of the dependent variable when the value of the independent variable equals 2?
A simple linear regression is a model of the relationship between:

options.A: one or more dependent variables and one or more independent variables.
options.B: one dependent variable and one or more independent variables.
options.C: one dependent variable and one independent variable.

questions.explanation: A simple linear regression is a model of the relationship between one dependent variable and one independent variable. A multiple regression is a model of the relationship between one dependent variable and more than one independent variable.

questions.question: A simple linear regression is performed to quantify the relationship between the return on the common stocks of medium-sized companies (mid-caps) and the return on the S&P 500 index, using the monthly return on mid-cap stocks as the dependent variable and the monthly return on the S&P 500 as the independent variable. The results of the regression are shown below: Coefficient Standard Error of Coefficient t-Value 1.71 2.950 Intercept 0.58 11.69 S&P 500 1.52 0.130 Coefficient of determination = 0.599 The strength of the relationship, as measured by the correlation coefficient, between the return on mid-cap stocks and the return on the S&P 500 for the period under study was:
questions.explanation: We are given the coefficient of determination of 0.599 (R) and are asked to find the correlation coefficient (r), which is the square root of the coefficient of determination for a simple regression: V0.599 = 0.774

questions.reading_name: Topics in Demand and Supply Analysis
questions.question: The demand for a product tends to be price inelastic if:

options.A: few good complements for the product are available.
options.B: few good substitutes for the product are available.
options.C: people spend a large share of their income on the product.

questions.explanation: If a large price change results in a small change in quantity demanded, demand is inelastic. Cigarettes are an example of a good with inelastic demand.

questions.reading_name: Topics in Demand and Supply Analysis
questions.question: If the demand curve for a given product is a straight line with a slope of -5, this indicates that:

options.A: elasticity is constant along the demand curve.
options.C: demand is more elastic at higher prices.

questions.explanation: Elasticities will be greater (in absolute value) at higher prices.

questions.reading_name: Topics in Demand and Supply Analysis
questions.question: A decrease in the price of Good Y can result in a decrease of the quantity of Good Y demanded by consumers if the substitution effect: is positive and the income effect is negative and larger than the substitution effect.

options.B: is negative and larger than the positive income effect.
options.C: and the income effect are negative.

questions.explanation: If the price of Good Y decreases, the substitution effect will have a positive impact on the quantity demanded of Good Y. Thus, the only way that quantity demanded of Good Y can decrease is if there is a negative income effect that is greater in magnitude than the substitution effect; i.e., if Good Y is a Giffen good.

questions.reading_name: Topics in Demand and Supply Analysis
questions.question: If quantity demanded increases 15% when the price drops 1%, demand for this good:
questions.explanation: Whenever quantity demanded for a good changes by a greater percentage than price, the price elasticity of demand will be greater than 1.0 and demand for the product is considered to be elastic.

questions.reading_name: Topics in Demand and Supply Analysis
questions.question: A firm in a perfectly competitive industry that seeks to maximize profit isnost likelyto continue production in the short run as long which of the following conditions exists? Price is equal to or greater than:
questions.explanation: If a firm is covering its average variable costs, it will continue to operate in the short run since it is covering some portion of its fixed costs.

questions.reading_name: Topics in Demand and Supply Analysis
questions.question: If quantity demanded increases 20% when the price drops 2%, this good exhibits:

options.A: inelastic, but not perfectly inelastic, demand.
options.B: elastic, but not perfectly elastic, demand.

questions.explanation: If quantity demanded increases 20% when the price drops 2%, this good exhibits elastic demand. Whenever demand changes by a greater percentage than price, demand is considered to be elastic.

questions.reading_name: Topics in Demand and Supply Analysis
questions.question: When two goods are complements, the cross elasticity of demand is:

options.A: negative, and for substitutes the cross price elasticity of demand is negative. ×
options.B: negative, and for substitutes the cross price elasticity of demand is positive.
options.C: positive, and for substitutes the cross price elasticity of demand is negative.

questions.explanation: The cross elasticity of demand for goods that are complements is negative because an increase in the price of one would tend to decrease the quantity demanded of the other. The cross elasticity of demand for substitute goods is positive because an increase in the price of one would tend to increase the quantity demanded of the other. (Module 8.1, LOS

questions.reading_name: Topics in Demand and Supply Analysis
questions.question: A firm that is experiencing diseconomies of scale should:

options.B: decrease output in the short run.
options.C: shut down in the long run.

questions.explanation: If a firm is experiencing diseconomies of scale, it should decrease its plant size to the efficient scale, which is the size that minimizes long-run average total cost. Plant size can be adjusted in the long run but not in the short run.

questions.reading_name: Topics in Demand and Supply Analysis
questions.question: A company has estimated that the price elasticity of demand for its output is -1.1. If the company increases the price of its product by 5%, it ismost likelythat:

options.A: both total revenue and profits will decrease.
options.B: total revenue will decrease but profits may increase.
options.C: total revenue will increase but profits may decrease.

questions.explanation: Price elasticity of -1.1 tells us that a 5% increase in price will reduce sales by more than 5%, so total revenue will decrease. Whether profits increase or decrease will depend on whether the cost reduction from producing less output is greater or less than the decrease

questions.reading_name: Topics in Demand and Supply Analysis
questions.question: When household incomes go down and the quantity of a product demanded goes up, the product is:
questions.explanation: When household incomes go down and the quantity demanded of a product goes up, the product is an inferior good. Inferior goods include things like bus travel and margarine.

questions.reading_name: Topics in Demand and Supply Analysis
questions.question: A good is considered an inferior good if it exhibits a negative:
questions.explanation: The income effect is negative for an inferior good. An increase in income results in a decrease in the quantity demanded.

questions.reading_name: Topics in Demand and Supply Analysis
questions.question: If a good has elastic demand, a small percentage price increase will cause:

options.A: a larger percentage decrease in the quantity demanded.
options.B: a smaller percentage increase in the quantity demanded.
options.C: a larger percentage increase in the quantity demanded.

questions.explanation: If a good has elastic demand, a small price increase will cause a larger decrease in the quantity demanded. Demand is elastic when the percentage change in quantity demanded is larger than the percentage change in price.

questions.reading_name: Topics in Demand and Supply Analysis
questions.question: Which of the following most accuratelydescribes economies of scale? Economies of scale:

options.A: are dependent on short-run average costs.
options.C: occur when long-run unit costs fall as output increases.

questions.explanation: Economies of scale occur when the percentage increase in output is greater than the percentage increase in the cost of all inputs. Economies of scale occur over the range where the long-run average cost curve slopes downward.

questions.reading_name: Topics in Demand and Supply Analysis
questions.question: The percent change in demand for a good divided by the percent change in the price of another good is known as the:
questions.explanation: Percent change in quantity demanded Cross price elasticity of demand = Percent change in price of another good

questions.reading_name: Topics in Demand and Supply Analysis
questions.question: Income elasticity is defined as the percentage change in:

options.A: income divided by the percentage change in the quantity demanded.
options.B: quantity demanded divided by the percentage change in income. quantity demanded divided by the percentage change in the price of the

questions.explanation: Income elasticity is defined as the percentage change in quantity demanded divided by the percentage change in income. Normal goods have positive values for income elasticity, and inferior goods have negative income elasticity.

questions.reading_name: Topics in Demand and Supply Analysis
questions.question: The law of diminishing returns states that for a given production process, as more and more of a resource (such as labor) are added, holding the quantities of other resources fixed:

options.A: output increases at a decreasing rate.
options.B: cost declines at a decreasing rate.
options.C: cost declines at an increasing rate.

questions.explanation: The law of diminishing returns states that for a given production process, as more ano more resources (such as labor) are added holding the quantities of other resources fixed, output increases at a decreasing rate. This occurs because, at some point, adding more workers results in inefficiencies.

questions.reading_name: Topics in Demand and Supply Analysis
questions.question: Price elasticity of demand ismost accurately defined as the change in:

options.A: market price in response to a change in the quantity demanded.
options.B: quantity demanded in response to a change in income.
options.C: quantity demanded in response to a change in market price.

questions.explanation: Price elasticity of demand is the percent change in quantity demanded relative to a percent change in price.

questions.reading_name: Topics in Demand and Supply Analysis
questions.question: If the price elasticity of demand is -1.5 and the price of the product increases 2%, the quantity demanded will:
questions.explanation: If the price elasticity of demand is -1.5, and you increase the price of the product 2%, the quantity demanded will decrease approximately 3%. When the price elasticity is negative, it means that price and demand move in opposite directions. Given a price decrease, demand will increase and vice versa. The absolute value, 1.5, indicates that demand will move one-and-a-half times as much as price.

questions.reading_name: Topics in Demand and Supply Analysis
questions.question: If the price elasticity of demand is -1.5 and a change in the price of the product increases the quantity demanded by 4%, then what is the percent change in price?
questions.explanation: Price elasticity of demand is calculated by dividing the percent change in quantity demanded by the percent change in price. The percent change in price is, therefore, the percent change in quantity demanded divided by the price elasticity of demand = 4/-1.5 =

questions.reading_name: Topics in Demand and Supply Analysis
questions.question: Based on the concept of diminishing returns, as the quantity of output increases, the short- run marginal costs of production eventually:
questions.explanation: The law of diminishing returns states that as more variable resources are a production process combined with a fixed input, output will eventually increase at a decreasing rate. In the short run, as the quantity produced rises, costs rise at an increasing rate.

questions.reading_name: Topics in Demand and Supply Analysis
questions.question: If the price elasticity of demand for a good is -4.0, then a 10% increase in price would result in a:
questions.explanation: Price elasticity of demand = (% change in Q demanded / % change in price). Given the price elasticity of demand and the percentage change in price, we can solve for the percentage change in quantity demanded = price elasticity of demand x percentage change in price. Here, -4.0 × 10% = -40%.

questions.reading_name: Topics in Demand and Supply Analysis
questions.question: If a good has elastic demand, a small price decrease will cause:

options.A: a larger decrease in the quantity demanded.
options.B: a larger increase in quantity demanded.
options.C: no change in the quantity demanded.

questions.explanation: If a good has elastic demand, a small price decrease will cause a larger increase in the quantity demanded.

questions.reading_name: Topics in Demand and Supply Analysis
questions.question: The price of milk in a country increases from €1.00 per liter to €1.10 per liter, and the quantity supplied does not change. This suggests the elasticity of the short-run supply of milk in this country is equal to:

options.A: infinity, and supply is perfectly elastic.
options.B: infinity, and supply is perfectly inelastic.
options.C: zero, and supply is perfectly inelastic.

questions.explanation: If quantity supplied does not respond to a change in price, supply is perfectly inelastic. For

questions.reading_name: Topics in Demand and Supply Analysis
questions.question: With respect to utility theory, the income effect for a decrease in the price of a good:

options.A: may increase or decrease consumption of the good.
options.B: will increase consumption of the good.
options.C: will decrease consumption of the good.

questions.explanation: The income effect for a decrease in price may be positive (for a normal good) or negative (for an inferior good). Therefore, the income effect from a price decrease may be to increase or decrease consumption of a good.

questions.reading_name: Topics in Demand and Supply Analysis
questions.question: The daily demand curve for olive oil (in liters) for a particular distributor is estimated as: Price olive oil = 20 - Qolive oil / 150 At a price of $10 per liter, the price elasticity of demand for olive oil islosestto:
questions.explanation: The demand function for olive oil is Q = 3000 - 150 P. At a price of 10, Q = 3000 - 150(10) = 1500.

questions.reading_name: Topics in Demand and Supply Analysis
questions.question: With respect to utility theory, the substitution effect for a decrease in the price of a good:

options.A: will decrease consumption of the good.
options.B: may increase or decrease consumption of the good.
options.C: will increase consumption of the good.

questions.explanation: In utility theory, if the price of one good decreases, the substitution effect causes consumption of that good to increase.

questions.reading_name: Topics in Demand and Supply Analysis
questions.question: A good for which consumers exhibit a negative income effect that is smaller than the substitution effect is most accuratelydescribed as a(n):
questions.explanation: For an inferior good the income effect is negative. A Giffen good is an inferior good for which the negative income effect is larger than the positive substitution effect, resulting in a decrease in consumption in response to a decrease in price. A Veblen good is not an inferior good, but rather a good that provides more utility to a consumer at a higher price than it provides at a lower price because the status benefits of ownership are greater at higher prices.

questions.reading_name: Topics in Demand and Supply Analysis
questions.question: Which of the following statements regarding diminishing marginal returns isnost accurate?

options.A: The total cost curve arches downward.
options.B: As the quantity produced rises, costs begin to rise at an increasing rate.
options.C: As the quantity produced rises, costs begin to rise at a decreasing rate.

questions.explanation: At production levels that are consistent with decreasing marginal returns, costs will increase at an increasing rate as production rises.

questions.reading_name: Topics in Demand and Supply Analysis
questions.question: The upward sloping segment of a long-run average total cost curve represents the existence of:
questions.explanation: Diseconomies of scale occur along the upward sloping segment of the long-run average total cost curve where costs rise as output increases. The flat portion at the bottom of the long-run average total costs curve represents constant returns to scale.

questions.reading_name: Topics in Demand and Supply Analysis
questions.question: When demand for a good is relatively inelastic, a higher price will:

options.A: fail to reduce the quantity demanded for the good.
options.B: have no impact on the demand for the good.
options.C: lead to an increase in total expenditures for the good.

questions.explanation: When demand is relatively inelastic, consumers do not reduce their quantity demanded very much when the price increases. That is, a given percentage increase in price results in a smaller percentage reduction in quantity demanded. Thus, total expenditures on the good increase. "Fail to reduce the quantity demanded for the good" is inaccurate because that would only be true if demand wasperfectlyinelastic.

questions.reading_name: Topics in Demand and Supply Analysis
questions.question: If the price of World Cup Soccer tickets increases from $40 a ticket to $50 a ticket and the quantity demanded of tickets stays the same, demand for the tickets is:
questions.explanation: Since the quantity of tickets demanded stayed the same after the price changed, the demand curve would have to be vertical which is a perfectly inelastic demand curve.

questions.reading_name: Topics in Demand and Supply Analysis
questions.question: For a linear demand curve, at the price where elasticity is -2.0, reducing prices will:

options.A: decrease total revenue and we are not at the point of maximum total revenue. x
options.B: increase total revenue and we are at the point of maximum total revenue.
options.C: increase total revenue and we are not at the point of maximum total revenue.

questions.explanation: If the price elasticity of demand is -2.0, this indicates that the percentage change in quantity demanded is twice the percentage change in price. Thus, a decrease in price will be more than offset by the increase in quantity, and total revenue will increase. We are not at the point of maximum total revenue which is where elasticity is - 1.0-the point of unit elastic demand.

questions.reading_name: Topics in Demand and Supply Analysis
questions.question: Which of the following ismost likelyto cause a decrease in the consumption of a good in response to a decline in the price of the good?
questions.explanation: The income effect can be negative if the good is an inferior good. The substitution effect is always positive and will cause consumption of a good to increase if the price declines. The law of demand assumes that a decrease in the price of a good will cause an increase in the quantity demanded.

questions.reading_name: Topics in Demand and Supply Analysis
questions.question: If the price elasticity of demand is -2 and the price of the product decreases by 5%, the quantity demanded will:
questions.explanation: If the price elasticity of demand is -2, and the price of the product decreases by 5%, the quantity demanded will increase 10%. The value -2 indicates that the percentage increase in the quantity demanded will be twice the percentage decrease in price.

questions.question: Concentration measures aremost likelyto be used to:

options.A: analyze barriers to entry into an industry.
options.B: identify the market structure of an industry.
options.C: measure elasticity of demand facing an industry.

questions.explanation: Concentration measures are used to identify the market structure of an industry (perfect competition, monopolistic competition, oligopoly, or monopoly). Concentration measures do not directly indicate an industry's barriers to entry or elasticity of demand.

questions.question: The most effective way to assess the impact of a potential merger on the market structure of an industry is to:
questions.explanation: The Herfindahl-Hirschman Index is more sensitive to mergers than the n-firm concentration ratio. Although barriers to entry for an industry are important in assessing market structure, they are not necessarily related to the impact of a merger.

questions.question: Firms in perfectly competitive markets and firms operating in a market characterized by monopolistic competition have several things in common. Which of the following iseast likely one of them? Both:

options.C: operate in markets that have low or no barriers to entry.

questions.explanation: The only item listed in the question that monopolistic competition and pure competition do not have in common is a perfectly elastic demand curve. Under pure competition, producers face a perfectly elastic demand curve, whereas price searchers face downward sloping demand curves.

questions.question: Which of the following isleast likelyto be considered a feature that is common to both monopolistic competition and perfect competition?

options.B: Low or no barriers to entry.
options.C: Zero economic profits in the long run.

questions.explanation: The only item listed in the question that monopolistic competition and perfect competition do not have in common is the use of advertising to differentiate their products. Extensive advertising is a key feature of monopolistic competition.

questions.question: In the dominant firm model of oligopoly, it isleast likelythat one firm:

options.A: is the innovation leader in product development.
options.B: has a significant cost advantage over its competitors.
options.C: effectively sets the price in the market.

questions.explanation: The dominant firm model of oligopoly is based on the assumption that one firm has a significant cost advantage which allows it to set the price in the market and control a relatively large share of the industry's production and sales. It does not assume that the firm will be the innovation leader in product development. In fact, being more innovative is one of the factors that allow smaller competitors that work at a cost disadvantage to

questions.question: When a firm operates under conditions of perfect competition, marginal revenue always equals:
questions.explanation: When a firm operates under conditions of perfect competition, marginal revenue always equals price. This is because, in perfect competition, price is constant (a horizontal line) so that marginal revenue is constant.

questions.question: Which of the following describes the regulatory practice of setting prices at a level where the monopoly firm's average total cost curve intersects the demand curve?
questions.explanation: Under average cost pricing, regulators attempt to force monopolies to reduce prices to where a firm's average total cost curve intersects the market demand curve. This will increase output and decrease price, increase allocative efficiency, and ensure zero economic profit.

questions.question: Which of the following isleast accurateregarding the relationship between price (P), marginal revenue (MR), average total cost (ATC), and marginal cost (MC) at the profit maximizing output under monopoly?
questions.explanation: To maximize profit, all firms expand output until marginal revenue equals marginal cost. Price is determined from the demand curve, which is above the marginal revenue curve since a monopoly faces a downward sloping demand curve.

questions.question: To benefit from price discrimination, a monopolisteast likelyneeds to have:

options.A: a way to prevent reselling between types of consumers.
options.B: a higher-quality product at a premium price and a lower-quality alternative. two identifiable groups of consumers with different price elasticities of deman‹

questions.explanation: Price discrimination involves a single product, not two alternatives. As long as the company faces a downward-sloping demand curve, can identify at least two groups of customers with different price elasticities of demand, and can prevent reselling between

questions.question: Which one of the following ismost likelyto contribute to the presence of monopoly in an industry? Inefficiency attributable to bureaucratic decision-making procedures in the industry.

options.C: Legal barriers to entry into the industry.

questions.explanation: An example of an industry with legal barriers is utility firms, which are granted exclusive rights to supply electricity in certain areas.

questions.question: A firm operating as a price taker will produce the quantity at which:
questions.explanation: A firm operating as a price taker will produce the quantity where MC = MR. It will maximize profit and not revenue. In the long run, it will make zero economic profit.

questions.question: What is the relationship between price and marginal revenue for a price searcher?
questions.explanation: For a price searcher, demand is downward sloping, marginal revenue is less than price since price must be reduced to sell additional units of output.

questions.question: Which of the following ismost likelyto be a characteristic of an oligopolistic industry?
questions.explanation: An oligopolistic industry exhibits a high degree of interdependence among firms. One firm's pricing decisions or advertising activities will affect the other firms' demand curves. These industries typically consist of a small number of sellers and have significant barriers to entry.

questions.question: Which of the following ismost likelya characteristic of monopolistic competition?

options.B: Each producer offers a differentiated product.

questions.explanation: Differentiated products are a feature of monopolistic competition markets. Interdependence is a characteristic of oligopoly markets. Horizontal demand curves facing

questions.question: Which of the following ismost likelya characteristic of perfect competition?

options.A: Different firms sell their output at different prices.
options.B: Barriers to entry are not a significant factor.
options.C: The number of firms in the market is small.

questions.explanation: Under perfect competition there are no significant barriers to entry into the market. An industry that can be characterized as perfect competition typically consists of a large number of firms, each of which can sell its entire output at the market price, and none of which are large enough to affect the market price.

questions.question: In a natural monopoly:

options.A: one firm controls all natural resources.
options.B: the average total cost of production continually declines with increased output C the price charged by a monopolist is determined by the intersection of the
options.C: demand curve with the marginal cost curve.

questions.explanation: A monopoly situation in which the average total cost of production continually declines with increased output is called a natural monopoly.

questions.question: Which of the following is/east accurate with regard to advertising for firms operating under monopolistic competition?

options.A: Advertising expenses are high relative to perfect competition and monopoly. x
options.B: Advertising may decrease average total cost. The increase to average total costs associated with advertising increases as output increases.

questions.explanation: Advertising expenses are high for firms in monopolistic competition. Not only because firms need to inform consumers about the unique features of a firm's products, but also to create or increase a perception of differences between products that are actually quite similar. Advertising costs increase average total costs, but the increase to average total cost attributable to advertising decreases as output increases because more fixed advertising dollars are being averaged over a larger quantity. If advertising increases output (sales) significantly, it can actually decrease a firm's average total cost if there are economies of scale.

questions.question: If a profit maximizing firm finds that its marginal revenue exceeds its marginal cost, it should increase output:

options.A: if it is a price searcher, but not if it is a price taker.
options.B: if it is a price taker, but not if it is a price searcher.
options.C: regardless of whether it is a price taker or a price searcher.

questions.explanation: Any firm will maximize profits by producing the output where MR = MC.

questions.question: The short-run supply curve to a firm operating under perfect competition isnost accurately described as the segment of the:

options.A: average total cost (ATC) curve above the average variable cost (AVC) curve.
options.B: marginal cost (MC) curve above the average variable cost (AVC) curve.
options.C: marginal cost (MC) curve below the average total cost (ATC) curve.

questions.explanation: The short-run supply curve for a firm under perfect competition is the segment of its MC curve above the AVC curve.

questions.question: Under perfect competition, a firm will be inclined to increase output as long as which of the following conditions exists?

options.A: Marginal revenue is greater than marginal cost.
options.B: Marginal revenue is greater than the average cost.
options.C: Marginal cost is less than average cost.

questions.explanation: A firm will continue to expand output as long as it is possible to earn an economic profit. In other words, a firm will expand output as long as marginal revenue is greater than marginal cost.

questions.question: The demand curves faced by monopolistic competitors is:

options.A: elastic due to the availability of many close substitutes.
options.B: inelastic due to the availability of many complementary goods.
options.C: not sensitive to price due to absence of close substitutes.

questions.explanation: The demand for products from monopolistic competitors is elastic due to the availability of many close substitutes. If a firm increases its product price, it will lose customers to firms selling substitute products.

questions.question: Monopolists will maximize profit by producing at an output level where which of the following conditions exists?

options.A: Price = demand = marginal revenue = marginal cost.

questions.explanation: To maximize profit, monopolists will expand output until marginal revenue equals marginal cost. Price will be greater than marginal revenue because a monopolist faces a downward sloping demand curve.

questions.question: Which of the following is/east likelyto be considered a necessary condition for a monopolist to realize profits from price discrimination?

options.A: A product for which the demand curve is downward sloping.
options.B: The ability to prevent trading between customers in different price groups.

questions.explanation: Price discrimination works when the seller (discriminator) faces a downward-sloping demand curve and has at least two customer groups each having different price elasticities for the product. It is also necessary that trading does not occur between customer groups so that the customers paying a lower price cannot resell the product to the customers paying a higher price.

questions.question: The type of economic market that features a large number of competitors offering differentiated products isbest characterized as:
questions.explanation: Monopolistic competition is used to describe markets where there are a large number of competitors producing differentiated products. In perfect competition all firms produce identical products. In an oligopoly there is a smal number of firms.

questions.question: In order for effective price discrimination to occur the seller must:

options.A: maximize revenue by selling at the highest price possible.
options.B: face a demand curve with a negative slope. have more than one identifiable group of customers with the same price
options.C: elasticities of demand for the product.

questions.explanation: In order for effective price discrimination to occur, the seller must have a downward sloping demand curve. The seller must also have at least two identifiable groups of customers with price elasticities of demand for the product, and the seller must be able to prevent customers from reselling the product.

questions.question: A perfectly competitive firm will choose to produce the quantity for which:

options.B: the marginal cost is less than marginal revenue.
options.C: the market price is equal to its marginal cost.

questions.explanation: A perfectly competitive firm will tend to expand its output so long as the market price is greater than marginal cost. In the short term and long term, profit is maximized when P =

questions.question: Which of the following ismost accuratefor a price-taker firm in long-run equilibrium when there are no barriers to entry?
questions.explanation: For a price-taker firm, long-run equilibrium is where P = MC = ATC. For price taking firms, P = MC. Competition eliminates economic profits in the long run so that P = ATC.

questions.question: Which of the following is themost likelyresult of a technological improvement in a perfectly competitive industry?

options.A: The industry supply curve shifts to the right.
options.B: Individual firms' supply curves shift to the left.
options.C: The costs for individual firms increase.

questions.explanation: When individual firms implement technological change, their costs decline and their supply (cost) curve shifts to the right. At the lower costs, firms are willing to supply a given quantity at a reduced price. The lower cost structure for the individual firms shifts the industry supply curve to the right.

questions.question: One way in which monopolistic competition can be distinguished from perfect competition is that in monopolistic competition:

options.A: each firm faces a perfectly elastic demand curve.
options.B: marginal revenue is greater than marginal cost at the quantity produced.
options.C: price is greater than marginal cost.

questions.explanation: In monopolistic competition, price is greater than marginal cost (i.e., firms can realize a markup). In perfect competition, P = MC. Firms in monopolistic competition are price searchers, i.e., each firm faces a downward sloping demand curve. Regardless of the market structure, all firms produce the quantity at which marginal revenue equals marginal cost.

questions.question: The kinked demand model assumes that at prices above the current price, the demand curve becomes:

options.A: less elastic because competitors will not increase their prices.
options.B: more elastic because competitors will increase their prices.
options.C: more elastic because competitors will not increase their prices.

questions.explanation: The kinked demand model of oligopoly behavior assumes that a firm's competitors will not match a price increase, but will match the price of a competitor that offers a lower price. The result is a demand curve that is more elastic above the current price, but less elastic below it.

questions.question: The kinked demand model assumes that below the current price, the demand curve becomes:

options.A: less elastic because competitors will decrease their prices.
options.B: less elastic because competitors will not decrease their prices.
options.C: more elastic because competitors will decrease their prices.

questions.explanation: The kinked demand model of oligopoly behavior assumes that a firm's competitors will not match a price increase, but will match the price of a competitor that offers a lower price. The result is a demand curve that is more elastic above the current price, but less elastic

questions.question: A monopolist will continue expanding output as long as:

options.B: marginal revenue is greater than marginal cost.
options.C: economic profit is greater than zero.

questions.explanation: The optimum behavior of all firms is to produce until the point where MR = MC. So, the monopolist can increase total profit by increasing production as long as marginal revenue is greater than marginal costs.

questions.question: Which of the following isleast relevant when explaining why monopoly firms can earn positive economic profits over the long term?

options.A: The ability to use price discrimination.
options.B: The existence of economies of scale.

questions.explanation: High entry barriers due to economies of scale, government licensing, resource controls, and patents prevent new firms from entering the market to exploit positive economic profit opportunities.

questions.question: Firms in a perfectly competitive industry will increase their output until which of the following conditions is met?

options.A: Marginal revenue equals average total cost.

questions.explanation: When a firm operates under conditions of perfect competition, marginal revenue always equals price. Under perfect competition, price is constant (a horizontal line) so marginal revenue is constant. Therefore a firm will increase output until marginal cost equals price.

questions.question: A market has the following characteristics: • There is a large number of independent sellers. • Each produces a differentiated product. • There are low barriers to entry. • Producers face downward-sloping demand curves. • Demand is highly elastic. This market is best characterized as:
questions.explanation: These conditions characterize monopolistic competition. By contrast, monopolies and oligopolies have high barriers to entry and involve either a single seller (monopoly) or a small number of interdependent sellers (oligopoly).

questions.question: Firms in an industry characterized by perfect competition exit the market because they are experiencing economic losses. What will be the effect on the equilibrium market price and the effect on the total revenue of a firm that remains in the industry in the short run? Price Revenue
questions.explanation: If firms in an industry are experiencing losses, some will exit. This will decrease industry supply and increase equilibrium market price. The remaining firms in the industry will increase production along their individual supply curves (short-run marginal cost curves) and increase production at the higher price. The combination of a higher price and greater quantity produced will cause total revenues to increase for the remaining firms. (Module

questions.question: When a regulatory agency requires a monopolist to use average cost pricing, the intent is to produce the quantity where the:

options.A: the market demand curve intersects the average total cost curve.
options.B: marginal revenue curve intersects the marginal cost curve.
options.C: average total cost curve intersects the marginal revenue curve.

questions.explanation: When a regulatory agency requires a monopolist to use average cost pricing, the intent is to price the product where the average total cost curve intersects the market demand curve. There are problems in using this method, e.g., determining exactly what the average total cost really is.

questions.question: Which of the following regarding monopolistic competition ismost accurate?

options.A: Each firm produces a differentiated product.
options.B: There are very few independent sellers.
options.C: Zero barriers to entry and exit exist.

questions.explanation: Other characteristics of monopolistic competition (also known as competitive price searcher markets) are: a large number of independent sellers, low barriers to entry, and an elastic downward sloping demand curve.

questions.question: A monopolist will expand production until:

options.A: P = MC and the price of the product will be determined by the MC curve.
options.B: MR = MC and the price of the product will be determined by the demand curve V
options.C: MR = MC and the price of the product will be determined by the MR curve.

questions.explanation: A monopolist will expand production until MR = MC. The demand curve lies above the intersection of the MR and MC curve and the price charged is the price on the demand curve for the output where MR = MC.

questions.question: In a study seminar, the following comments were made: Comment 1: "In the short run, an increase in demand in a perfectly competitive industry will result in negative economic profit for some firms in the industry." Comment 2: "In the long run, a permanent increase in demand in a perfectly competitive industry will result in zero economic profit for the firms in the industry." With respect to these comments:
questions.explanation: Comment 1 is incorrect because an increase in industry demand will increase equilibrium price and output. At the higher price, firms will earn positive economic profits in the short run because the higher price will exceed average total cost. Over the long run, however, new firms will enter the market to exploit the positive economic profits, causing prices to decline until all firms are again earning zero economic profit.

questions.question: A market structure characterized by a large number of firms all producing identical products is best described as:
questions.explanation: In a perfectly competitive economic market, there are many independent firms, each seller is small relative to the total market, and there are no barriers to entry or exit.

questions.question: Which of the following isleast likelyto be considered a reason why regulation of monopolies is not effective?

options.A: Regulators do not know the firm's cost structure.
options.B: Regulation shifts industry demand and increases prices.
options.C: Regulation reduces the incentive for firms to reduce costs.

questions.explanation: Regulation is not associated with a shift in industry demand.

questions.question: Price discrimination is most accurately defined by which of the following? Price discrimination is the practice of charging different consumers different prices for:

options.B: similar products that have different price elasticities of demand.
options.C: similar products that have identical per-unit production costs.

questions.explanation: Price discrimination is the practice of charging different consumers different prices for the same product or service. Examples include different prices for airline tickets based on whether a Saturday-night stay is involved and different prices for movie tickets based on age.

questions.question: The short-run supply curve for a firm under perfect competition is the firm's:

options.A: average variable cost curve above marginal revenue.
options.B: marginal cost curve above average total cost.
options.C: marginal cost curve above average variable cost.

questions.explanation: The supply curve for a firm under perfect competition is its marginal cost curve above average variable cost. As long as price exceeds AVC, the firm will produce up to the

questions.question: Natural monopolies exist because they can produce at lower costs with greater output, which means there are economies of scale. Which of the following industries is typically a natural monopoly?
questions.explanation: With a natural monopoly average costs of production will be lowest when a single large firm produces the entire output demanded such as a utility.

questions.question: Which of the following statements is/east accurate with regard to the efficiency of monopolistic competition?

options.A: Consumers benefit from brand name promotion and advertising. C
options.B: Monopolistic competition is at least as efficient as perfect competition. The expense of advertising and promotion may not be justified by their benefit to consumers.

questions.explanation: The efficiency of monopolistic competition is unclear. Consumers may make better purchasing decisions due to the information content of brand name promotion and advertising. However, there are those that argue that the increased cost of advertising and sales is not justified by the benefits of these activities and represent inefficient use of resources.

questions.question: Which of the following isleast likelya necessary condition for a monopolist to realize increased profits from price discrimination?

options.A: A product for which the demand curve is downward sloping.
options.C: The ability to prevent trading between customers in different price groups.

questions.explanation: Price discrimination works when the seller (discriminator) faces a downward-sloping demand curve and has at least two customer groups each having different price elasticities of demand for the product. It is also necessary that trading does not occur between customer groups so that the customers paying a lower price cannot resell the product to the customers paying a higher price.

questions.question: The short-run supply curve for a price taker firm is the portion of the marginal cost (MC) curve:

options.A: above the average total cost (ATC) curve.
options.B: above the average variable cost (AVC) curve.
options.C: below the average variable cost (AVC) curve.

questions.explanation: The short-run supply curve for a firm is its MC curve above the AVC curve. Price takers will produce where price (P) equals MC. At prices below the AVC curve the firm will not be able to remain in operation. Above the ATC curve the firm is making economic profits and will continue to expand production along the MC curve.

questions.question: Which of the following isleast likelya barrier to entry?
questions.explanation: Often barriers to entry are government licensing and legal barriers.

questions.question: A monopolist will maximize profits by:

options.A: producing at the point where price is equal to MR. producing at the output level where marginal revenue equals average variable
options.B: cost and charging a price along the demand curve that corresponds to the output rate.
options.C: Producing at the output level where marginal revenue equals marginal cost an charging a price on the demand curve that corresponds to the output rate.

questions.explanation: A monopolist will maximize profits by producing at the output level where marginal revenue equals marginal cost and charging a price on the demand curve that corresponds to the output rate.

questions.question: Monopolistic competition differs from pure monopoly in that:

options.A: monopolistic competitors are price takers and monopolists are not.
options.B: monopolistic competitors have low barriers to entry and monopolists do not.
options.C: monopolists maximize profits and monopolistic competitors do not.

questions.explanation: Another name for monopolistic competition is a competitive price searcher market. Monopolistic competition refers to a large number of independent sellers, each produces a differentiated product, each market has a low barrier to entry, and each producer faces a downward sloping demand curve.

questions.question: Which one of the following structures is characterized by free entry and exit, a differentiated product, and price searcher behavior?
questions.explanation: Monopolistic competition is another name for competitive price-searcher markets. There are a large number of independent sellers, each produces a differentiated product, each market has a low barrier to entry, and each producer faces a downward sloping demand curve.

questions.question: Which of the following isleast likelya barrier to entry?
questions.explanation: Few sellers are a characteristic not a barrier, of a price-searcher market where there are high barriers to entry. Other barriers are patents or exclusive rights of production.

questions.question: The practice of charging different consumers different prices for the same product or service is called:
questions.explanation: The practice of charging different consumers different prices for the same product or service is called price discrimination.

questions.question: Which of the following isleast likelya characteristic of perfect competition?

options.A: The products produced within a given market are homogenous.
options.B: The demand curve for an individual firm is a vertical line.
options.C: The size of each firm is small relative to the size of the overall market.

questions.explanation: Under perfect competition individual firms have no control over price resulting in a demand schedule that is perfectly elastic or horizontal.

questions.question: A firm has the following characteristics: • relatively small in size. • marginal revenue is equal to the selling price. • economic profits will not be earned for any significant period of time. The firm is best describedas existing in a(n):
questions.explanation: The firm being described is a price taker firm in a purely competitive market. These firms must sell their product at the going market price, there are no barriers to entry, and there are a large number of firms that produce a homogeneous product.

questions.question: Oligopolists have an incentive to cheat on collusive agreements in order to:

options.A: increase their individual share of the joint profit.
options.B: restrict output and put upward pressure on price.

questions.explanation: Colluding restricts output and puts upward pressure on price, but cheating actually increases output and ultimately, if enough cheating occurs, puts downward pressure on

questions.question: Monopolistic competition differs from pure monopoly in that:

options.A: monopolists maximize profit; monopolistic competitors do not.
options.B: monopolistic competitors are price takers, monopolists are not. barriers to entry are high under monopoly, but low under monopolistic competition.

questions.explanation: Monopolistic competition is characterized by the low barriers to enter its competitive markets. In contrast, a monopoly exists only where there are high barriers to market entry.

questions.question: An oligopoly is least likelycharacterized by:
questions.explanation: Oligopolies consist of a small number of sellers. They tend to be characterized by barriers to entry such as significant economies of scale.

questions.question: In the long-run, a firm operating under perfect competition will:

options.B: produce a quantity at which marginal revenue is less than marginal cost.
options.C: face a perfectly inelastic demand curve.

questions.explanation: A firm operating under conditions of perfect competition will generate zero economic profit in the long run. Firms may generate economic profits in the short run, but due to the lack of entry barriers, new competitors will enter the market and prices will adjust downward until economic profits become zero. Firms in the industry are price takers (i.e., each firm faces a perfectly elastic demand curve).

questions.question: Under monopolistic competition, companies can earn positive economic profits in:

options.A: neither the short run nor the long run.
options.B: the short run and in the long run.
options.C: the short run but not in the long run.

questions.explanation: In a market characterized by monopolistic competition, companies can earn positive economic profits in the short run if the price of their product is greater than the average total cost of producing it. In the long run, because barriers to entry are low, economic profits will attract new entrants. Additional producers will drive the price lower until price equals average total cost, economic profit is zero, and new competitors no longer have an incentive to enter the market.

questions.question: Which of the following isleast likelya condition of a perfectly competitive market?
questions.explanation: The only item listed that is NOT a condition of a perfectly competitive market is that sellers make economic profits. In fact, sellers do not make economic profit after taking into account their opportunity costs.

questions.question: In the long-run, after all firms in a perfectly competitive industry have adopted new technology, the:

options.A: individual firm supply will increase as demand decreases.
options.B: price will be set where average variable cost is equal to marginal revenue.
options.C: price will equal minimum average total cost.

questions.explanation: After some firms in an industry adopt a technological change, the existing firms that use the old technology will experience losses and either adopt the technology or exit the industry. Long-run equilibrium with price equal to minimum average total cost for the new technology will be established.

questions.question: Which of the following is/east likelya characteristic of an oligopoly?

options.A: Products can either be similar or differentiated.

questions.explanation: Oligopolies have large economies of scale and interdependence among competitors.

questions.question: Which of the following statements about a monopolist iseast accurate?

options.A: Unlike an oligopolist, a monopolist will always be able to earn economic profit. C
options.B: The monopolist faces a downward sloping demand curve. A profit-maximizing monopolist will expand output until marginal revenue equals marginal cost.

questions.explanation: In some cases, a monopolist may be unable to sell for a profit. Price may be insufficient to cover the per-unit cost of the monopolist, even when operating at the MR = MC rate of output. The monopolist faces a downward-sloping demand curve.

questions.question: A perfect competition has all of the following characteristics EXCEPT:

options.B: a large number of independent firms.

questions.explanation: In a perfectly competitive market all the firms produce a homogeneous product.

questions.question: If an industry features differentiated products but has low barriers to entry, in the long run the firms in the industry will experience:
questions.explanation: An industry with low barriers to entry will produce zero economic profits in the long run.

questions.question: Statement 1: "The kinked demand curve model of oligopoly assumes that a decrease in price will not be followed by other firms in the industry, but a price increase will." Statement 2: "Firms in monopolistic competition have high advertising expenses because they want to create the perception that their product is different from their competitors' products when the competing products are actually quite similar." With respect to these statements:
questions.explanation: Statement 1 is incorrect because the kinked demand curve model contends that each firm in oligopoly competition believes that an increase (not decrease) in its price will not be followed by the competition, but a decrease (not increase) in price will. Each firm believes that it faces a demand curve that is more elastic (flatter) above a given price, i.e., the kink, than it is below the given price.

questions.question: Which of the following most accurately describes why firms under monopolistic competition face elastic demand for their products?
questions.explanation: The demand for products from firms competing in monopolistic competition is relatively elastic due to the availability of close substitutes. If a firm increases its product price, it will lose customers to firms selling slightly differentiated products at lower prices.

questions.question: Even though the producer surplus increases under a monopoly scenario, relative to one of perfect competition, the consumer surplus decreases by:
